OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of
 
 human immunodeficiency virus
 
 and
 
 hepatitis C virus
 
 coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones (Figures 1a and 1b). Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation (Figure 2a), while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (Figure 2b).
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones. Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation, while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Middle-aged male with HIV and hepatitis C coinfection (clinical context may be less relevant).  
- Hypoplastic fingernails detected on physical exam (clinical finding).  
- Bilateral bony lesions projecting from the posterior iliac bones.  
- Bilateral patellar hypoplasia with luxation seen on knee radiographs.  
- Bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature on elbow radiographs.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Nailâpatella syndrome (NPS):  
   - Pros: Classic triad includes nail dysplasia (hypoplastic nails), patellar hypoplasia/aplasia, and abnormalities of the iliac horns (bilateral bony projections from posterior iliac bones).  
   - Cons: None significant; findings match classic description.  

2) CoffinâSiris syndrome:  
   - Pros: Typically includes nail hypoplasia involving the fifth fingernail/little finger, developmental delay, and coarse facial features.  
   - Cons: No bone anomalies like iliac horns or patellar/radial head abnormalities are characteristic; joint dislocations not a hallmark.  

3) MeierâGorlin syndrome:  
   - Pros: Includes patellar aplasia/hypoplasia, and growth delay.  
   - Cons: Does not include iliac horns or radial head/radio-humeral dislocations; nail findings are not prominent.  

4) Coxopodopatellar syndrome:  
   - Pros: Includes patellar aplasia/hypoplasia, contractures, and other skeletal abnormalities.  
   - Cons: No mention of bilateral iliac horns or nail hypoplasia in classic description; radiologic features differ; considered part of the spectrum of NPS variants but more severe.  

5) Genitopatellar syndrome:  
   - Pros: Includes patellar agenesis/hypoplasia, genital anomalies, and renal anomalies.  
   - Cons: No iliac horns or nail hypoplasia; more systemic involvement; radiologic findings less specific.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Nailâpatella syndrome (exact match to key findings: hypoplastic nails, iliac horns, patellar hypoplasia with luxation, and radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation).  
2) Coxopodopatellar syndrome (similar skeletal features, but less specific nail involvement; syndrome is rarer and more severe).  
3) MeierâGorlin syndrome (patellar hypoplasia present, but lacks iliac horns and nail hypoplasia).  
4) Genitopatellar syndrome (patellar anomalies but no iliac horns or nail changes).  
5) CoffinâSiris syndrome (nail hypoplasia present but lacks bone anomalies like iliac horns or patellar/radial defects).  

Thus, the findings are pathognomonic for Nailâpatella syndrome.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Nailâpatella syndrome  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base. Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed. It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe. A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Angiosarcoma, Ig-G4 related disease, Primary cardiac lymphoma, Erdheim–Chester disease, Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Elderly male (81) with atrial fibrillation and new-onset orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea.  
- Labs: elevated troponins.  
- ECG: third-degree AV block.  
- Echo: right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.  
- PET-CT: large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue involving right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove; spares left ventricle; also hypermetabolic pulmonary nodule in left lung base.  
- Contrast-enhanced chest CT: infiltrative hypodense mass in myocardium involving right side chambers and interatrial septum; mass encircles right coronary artery; moderate pericardial effusion; solid nodule in left lower lobe confirmed.  
- Histology obtained from lung nodule (not provided here).  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Angiosarcoma:  
   - Pros: Most common primary cardiac malignancy, typically involves right atrium and right-sided chambers; can cause pericardial effusion; infiltrative mass and pericardial effusion are typical; hypermetabolic lesion on PET-CT fits malignancy; pulmonary metastasis (lung nodule) is common.  
   - Cons: None significant; fits well.  

2) Ig-G4 related disease:  
   - Pros: Can cause tumefactive lesions and myocardial infiltration; hypermetabolic on PET due to inflammation; pericardial effusion possible.  
   - Cons: Usually more diffuse, involves multiple organ systems; rarely forms a large mass with right atrial dominance; does not typically produce lung nodules; pulmonary nodules less common; less likely to cause third-degree AV block and marked myocardial infiltration sparing LV.  

3) Primary cardiac lymphoma:  
   - Pros: Can cause infiltrative cardiac masses; may involve right side; can cause AV block; often hypermetabolic on PET; pericardial effusion possible.  
   - Cons: Usually presents as a mass but more common in immunocompromised patients; lung metastasis less common; typically involve both ventricles or interatrial septum but less likely to involve only right chambers sparing LV entirely; elderly with normal immune status less typical.  

4) ErdheimâChester disease:  
   - Pros: Can involve pericardium and heart; pericardial effusion and myocardial involvement possible.  
   - Cons: Usually involves left-sided structures and bilateral perirenal involvement; infiltration pattern tends to be pericardial and coronary artery sheath thickening rather than large right-sided myocardial mass; rare to present as large hypermetabolic mass in the myocardium; lung nodule less typical; not typical for third-degree AV block.  

5) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS):  
   - Pros: Can occur as primary cardiac tumor; can cause large infiltrative mass; hypermetabolic on PET; may cause constitutional symptoms.  
   - Cons: Rare, more often involves left-side chambers or left atrium; lung metastasis possible but less typical; clinical presentation (AV block, right-sided involvement) less common; PET hypermetabolism fits but site and pattern less classic.  

C) Ranking (top to bottom):  
1) Angiosarcoma (best fit: right-sided large infiltrative hypermetabolic mass with lung metastasis, AV block, pericardial effusion).  
2) Primary cardiac lymphoma (possible, but less typical lung nodule and patient immune status).  
3) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (possible but less typical location).  
4) ErdheimâChester disease (less likely due to pattern and systemic involvement lacking).  
5) Ig-G4 related disease (least likely given mass characteristics and lung nodule).  

Thus, the best diagnosis is Angiosarcoma.  
</analysis>  
<final>Angiosarcoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- A 37-year-old man with high-grade fever, chills, vomiting, and epigastric abdominal pain for 7 days.  
- Laboratory: elevated TLC (18,500 cells/µL), high neutrophils (80%), and mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase (200 IU/L).  
- Imaging:  
  - USG: hepatomegaly, large left lobe liver abscess (~350 cc), signs of impending rupture. Mild intrahepatic biliary duct dilatation, intrahepatic venovenous collaterals between middle and right hepatic vein branches. Doppler shows echogenic content and absent flow at porto-splenic confluence, indicating thrombosis. Collateral formation at porta hepatis (cavernous transformation of portal vein).  
  - CECT: confirms abscess and thrombosis, pinching of IVC with small thrombus in the segment, pancreatic parenchymal varices in head and body of pancreas draining into splenic vein.  
  - MRCP: prominent intrahepatic bile ducts of left lobe, non-visualization of distal biliary channels consistent with obstruction.  
- Synthesis: Liver abscess with pylephlebitis (portal vein thrombosis secondary to infection) complicated by cavernous transformation. Presence of pancreatic parenchymal varices draining into splenic vein suggests portal hypertension and porto-systemic collateral formation, possibly secondary to portal vein thrombosis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation  
   - Pros: Pancreatic parenchymal varices and porto-systemic collaterals consistent with portal hypertension.  
   - Cons: No clinical or imaging evidence of cirrhosis (hepatomegaly, not atrophy or nodular surface; no typical nodularity or fibrosis). Acute abscess and pylephlebitis suggest a septic process, not chronic cirrhosis.  

2) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM)  
   - Pros: Intrahepatic venous collaterals noted.  
   - Cons: No mention of typical arteriovenous shunting, abnormal vascular tangle, or arterial phase findings on imaging. AVM does not explain liver abscess or thrombosis.  

3) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
   - Pros: Liver abscess present with confirmed portal vein thrombosis (pylephlebitis). Cavernous transformation and pancreatic parenchymal varices explain collateral formation secondary to portal hypertension from pylephlebitis. Imaging findings tightly match this diagnosis.  
   - Cons: None significant; best fit.  

4) Cystic lesions of pancreas  
   - Pros: Pancreatic involvement noted.  
   - Cons: No description of cystic lesions in pancreas, only varices. No imaging evidence for cystic neoplasm.  

5) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms  
   - Pros: Pancreatic ductal system possibly affected by obstruction.  
   - Cons: No mention of cystic dilation of pancreatic ducts or mucin-producing neoplasm; imaging findings do not suggest IPMN.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess (best fit);  
2) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices (less likely due to absence of cirrhosis signs and acute infection context);  
3) Arteriovenous malformation (not supported by imaging);  
4) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (no imaging support);  
5) Cystic lesions of pancreas (no cystic lesions described).  
</analysis><final> Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation. There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown). CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum, no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed germinal cell tumour, Pure yolk sac tumour, Seminoma, Choriocarcinoma, Teratoma, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 31-year-old male with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks.  
- Clinical exam shows large solid mass in right hemiscrotum, with penis retraction.  
- Extremely high alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) >53,000 µg/L (normal AFP is <10 µg/L).  
- Ultrasound: large heterogeneous right testicular mass with solid and necrotic/cystic components, high vascularity, no normal right testicular tissue or epididymis visualized.  
- Left testis and epididymis normal.  
- CT: confirms right testicular mass, no metastases or lymphadenopathy.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1–2 pros/cons each:  

1) Mixed germinal cell tumour:  
- Pros: Common in this age group; can elevate AFP if yolk sac component is present.  
- Cons: Mixed GCT usually shows a combination of histologic types; AFP elevation can be due to yolk sac component but we lack info on other components. No metastases yet.  
2) Pure yolk sac tumour:  
- Pros: Yolk sac tumour is a non-seminomatous GCT that typically produces very high AFP levels (often >10,000 µg/L); AFP >53,000 supports yolk sac.  
- Cons: Pure yolk sac tumors are less common in classic age group (pediatric/adolescent) but occur in young adults too.  
3) Seminoma:  
- Pros: Common testicular tumor in young adult males.  
- Cons: Seminomas typically do not elevate AFP (AFP is normal or slightly elevated only if there is non-seminomatous component). This case has extremely high AFP, essentially ruling out pure seminoma.  
4) Choriocarcinoma:  
- Pros: Can cause hemorrhagic necrosis and highly vascular mass.  
- Cons: Typically produces high beta-hCG, not AFP. No mention of elevated beta-hCG. AFP is not characteristic.  
5) Teratoma: 
- Pros: Can be part of mixed GCTs and cause solid and cystic mass.  
- Cons: Does not usually produce high AFP unless mixed with yolk sac or other non-seminomatous elements. No info on other tumor markers or elements.  
6) Lymphoma:  
- Pros: Can cause testicular mass in older patients.  
- Cons: Rare in young adults; does not produce AFP.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- Seminoma and choriocarcinoma are essentially excluded due to AFP profile.  
- Lymphoma excluded due to age and AFP.  
- Teratoma alone is unlikely given the extremely high AFP.  
- Mixed germ cell tumor (MGCN) vs pure yolk sac tumor. The key is AFP >53,000 µg/L, which is extraordinarily high, and yolk sac tumors are known to elevate AFP dramatically, often >10,000 µg/L. While mixed GCTs can elevate AFP, the magnitude in pure yolk sac tumor is higher and more typical.  

Therefore, the most specific diagnosis fitting age, presentation, imaging, and AFP level is pure yolk sac tumour.  
</analysis>  
<final>Pure yolk sac tumour</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 51-year-old female patient, a farmer, presented with two years history of abdominal pain, vomits, diarrhoea, asthenia, weight loss (12%), and arthralgias. Laboratory findings showed
 
 iron deficiency anaemia
 
 , increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate, mild leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, C-reactive protein 6 mg/dL
 
 (normal value < 0.5 mg/dL)
 
 and hypoalbuminemia 2.5 g/dL.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed cystadenocarcinoma of the right ovary with
 
 suspicion of lymph node metastases
 
 and the patient was referred to our oncology institute.
 
 The exams were re-assessed and the malignant ovarian tumour was excluded, as the MRI was highly suggestive of cystadenofibroma
 
 (Figure 1).
 
 In addition, the CT demonstrated low-attenuation lymph nodes (fatty infiltration) in the left supraclavicular region, mediastinum, retroperitoneum and mesentery
 
 (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 3a, and 3b). Furthermore, prominent jejunal folds were seen on T2-weighted images (Figure 4).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 51-year-old female farmer with chronic abdominal pain, gastrointestinal symptoms (vomiting, diarrhea), asthenia, weight loss, arthralgias.  
- Laboratory: iron-deficiency anemia, elevated ESR, mild leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, markedly elevated CRP, hypoalbuminemia.  
- Imaging: initial CT and MRI suggested right ovarian cystadenocarcinoma with possible lymph node metastases.  
- Reassessment on MRI retracted ovarian malignancy in favor of cystadenofibroma (benign).  
- Additional CT findings: low-attenuation (fatty infiltration) lymph nodes in left supraclavicular region, mediastinum, retroperitoneum, mesentery.  
- MRI: T2-weighted images show prominent jejunal folds.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Whipple’s disease:  
- Pro: Chronic abdominal symptoms, weight loss, arthralgias, laboratory inflammatory markers.  
- Con: Typical imaging findings include mesenteric lymphadenopathy, but fatty infiltration of lymph nodes is not classic. Also, jejunal thickening/wrinkles can be seen, but the fat infiltration of lymph nodes and extensive fatty nodes in thorax/abdomen is not typical.  

2) Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease:  
- Pro: Fatty infiltration of mesenteric lymph nodes is a hallmark; prominent jejunal folds due to villous atrophy or mucosal edema can fit celiac disease; systemic symptoms (weight loss, arthralgias), iron-deficiency anemia, elevated ERS/CRP.  
- Con: Extra-mesenteric low-attenuation nodes (supraclavicular, mediastinal) are less often reported but can occur in celiac-associated mesenteric lymph node fat replacement syndrome.

3) Lymphoma:  
- Pro: Low-attenuation lymphadenopathy and systemic inflammatory signs can be seen; common in retroperitoneum and mesentery.  
- Con: Fatty infiltration (low attenuation due to fat) of lymph nodes is not typical for lymphoma; lymphoma lymph nodes usually firm and hyperdense/enhancing; jejunal prominent folds less typical; absence of bulky masses or organomegaly argues against lymphoma; normal MRI for ovary after re-evaluation argues against metastasis; patient systemic signs more inflammatory than neoplastic.

4) Malignant lymph nodes:  
- Pro: Initial suspicion due to ovarian malignancy and lymphadenopathy.  
- Con: Same as lymphoma, lack of typical imaging features, fat infiltration argues against malignancy; re-assessment excluded primary malignancy; clinical presentation with inflammatory markers and fatty nodes refutes this.

C) Ranking:  
1) Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease (most consistent with fatty infiltration of mesenteric nodes, prominent jejunal folds, systemic inflammatory signs, and iron-deficiency anemia typical of malabsorption).  
2) Whipple's disease (possible but less typical imaging findings).  
3) Lymphoma (unlikely due to fat in nodes and systemic inflammatory labs).  
4) Malignant lymph nodes (refuted by MRI re-evaluation and imaging features).  
</analysis>  

<final>  
Cavitating mesenteric lymph node syndrome related to celiac disease  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

- MRI findings: Heterogeneous complex cystic lesion with thin wall; lesion hypointense on T2-weighted images and hyperintense on T1-weighted images; radiating septa, low signal on T2 and high signal on T1; septa delineate multiple smaller cysts (hypointense T1, hyperintense T2); no restricted diffusion; no significant soft tissue enhancement post-contrast.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  

1) Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb)  
- Pros: The description of a heterogeneous complex cystic lesion with septa and fluid densities is classic for hydatid cysts. Radiating septa and daughter cysts with different signal intensities on MRI are typical features. The presence of a thin wall and lack of significant enhancement is consistent. Clinical context (fever, malaise) fits subclinical hydatid disease.  
- Cons: The lesion is somewhat large but compatible. No mention of calcifications or typical hydatid membranes; biopsy showing fibro-hyalinised nodule is not diagnostic. However, the imaging is more characteristic than biopsy.  

2) Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis  
- Pros: Patient has lymphoma, can have hepatic involvement. Cystic or necrotic metastases can have fluid densities and heterogeneous appearance.  
- Cons: Usually metastatic lesions enhance or have peripheral rim enhancement, often show restricted diffusion on MRI. In this case, no restricted diffusion or significant enhancement, making this less likely. Also, biopsy did not show malignancy.  

3) Hepatic abscess  
- Pros: Fever, elevated CRP, heterogeneous fluid lesion with septa on imaging can be seen in abscesses.  
- Cons: No restricted diffusion (typical for abscess pus), biopsy didn't reveal inflammation or organisms. Clinical presentation is subacute with no severe infection signs. No mention of inflammatory signs on imaging.  

4) Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma  
- Pros: Typically multiloculated cystic lesion with septa; can be large.  
- Cons: Usually enhance septa or solid components, sometimes papillary projections; often in middle-aged women but usually no fever or systemic inflammatory signs. The lesion here shows no significant enhancement and no solid nodules, making this less likely.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb) – best fit with classic imaging features and clinical context.  
2) Cystic/necrotic liver metastasis – less likely due to lack of enhancement and negative biopsy.  
3) Hepatic abscess – unlikely due to lack of restricted diffusion and inflammation.  
4) Biliary cystadenoma/cystadenocarcinoma – less likely due to lack of enhancement and clinical signs.  

</analysis>  
<final>  
Hepatic hydatid disease (type IIb)  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A one-year-old full-term-born male child, born to non-consanguineous parents, presents with complaints of thoracic deformity. No history of previous surgeries.
 Imaging Findings: The AP chest radiograph (Figure 1) demonstrated the absence of ribs in the right mid-zone, with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs. Fusion of multiple ribs was also noted on the left side. The image showed evidence of dextroscoliosis.
 
 Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally (Videos and Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, 4, and 5).
 
 Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae (Figures 2b and 2c).
 
 Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.
PostDescription:

The image showed evidence of dextroscoliosis. Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally. Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae. Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.

- Absence of ribs in right mid-zone with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs on both sides.

- Fusion of posterior vertebral elements at multiple levels with dextroscoliosis of thoracic spine.  
- Posterior vertebral arch defects involving lumbar (L5) and sacral vertebrae.  
- Mild volume loss of right hemithorax without pulmonary parenchymal, bronchial or vascular abnormalities.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Spondylocostal dysostosis (Jarcho–Levin syndrome):  
- Pros: Presence of fused ribs and multiple vertebral malformations (unsegmented block vertebrae), rib fusion and bifid ribs are classic.  
- Cons: Typically involves vertebral segmentation defects but vertebral arch defects and sacral involvement are less common. Pulmonary hypoplasia can be mild.  

2) Spondylothoracic dysostosis (Lavy–Moseley syndrome):  
- Pros: Known for severe vertebral segmentation defects, extensive posterior vertebral element fusion ("crab-like" appearance), multiple rib fusions including severe and extensive involvement, vertebral arch defects more prominent. Also associated with thoracic deformity and pulmonary hypoplasia.  
- Cons: Usually presents with more severe respiratory compromise and thoracic deformity than described here; however presentation and findings (posterior vertebral arch defects, multiple rib fusions, dextroscoliosis) are quite characteristic.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Spondylothoracic dysostosis (Lavy–Moseley syndrome)  
2) Spondylocostal dysostosis (Jarcho–Levin syndrome)  

Rationale: The presence of posterior vertebral arch defects (L5 and all sacral vertebrae), extensive rib fusions including bifid ribs and segmental widening, and severe thoracic deformity with volume loss aligns more closely with spondylothoracic dysostosis, which is characterized by more severe vertebral defects and a distinctive rib and vertebral pattern. The bilateral rib fusions with bifid ribs and vertebral arch defects favor this diagnosis over spondylocostal dysostosis. However, the radiographic features share overlap, but the posterior arch defects and the pattern of rib fusions tilt the diagnosis towards spondylothoracic dysostosis.

 Imaging Findings: No abnormalities are evident on a PA radiograph of the chest (Figure 1). The CT reveals no thoracic abnormalities except for two bilateral cortical defects of the undersurface of the sternal extremity of each clavicle, being slightly larger on the left side (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). An MRI is additionally performed, revealing no evidence of a pathological fracture, soft tissue mass or bone marrow oedema (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: The chest radiograph revealed a presternal soft tissue swelling without calcifications and with no evidence of bony anomalies at the sternum, such as bone erosions or a periosteal reaction (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan showed a fusiform soft tissue density lesion, centred on the manubriosternal joint, involving both superficial and deep planes of the synchondrosis, with subcutaneous and mediastinal extension, measuring 46 x 27 x 37 mm. No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

   - Cons: None; this is a classic and textbook description of the findings.

4) Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome: Omphalocele fits but other features missing; no spinal or cloacal defects.  
5) Pentalogy of Cantrell: Overlaps with omphalocele but lacks cloacal and spinal defects.  

</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 85-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a rapidly growing mass on her left shoulder (Figure 1), with no history of trauma.
 
 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.
 Imaging Findings: A shoulder X-ray and ultrasound scan were performed in order to rule out malignancy.
 
 The X-ray (Figure 2) shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ). Additionally, the humeral head is seen cranially displaced, with subsequent narrowing of the subacromial space. Osseous degenerative change can be seen in both the humeral head and the ACJ.
 
 On the US exam (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.
PostDescription:

Additionally, the humeral head is seen cranially displaced, with subsequent narrowing of the subacromial space. Osseous degenerative change can be seen in both the humeral head and the ACJ. On the US exam, the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Acromioclavicular joint traumatic injury, Degenerative acromioclavicular joint changes, Acromioclavicular cyst, Soft-tissue tumour, Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear
FinalDiagnosis: Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Elderly woman with rapidly growing, hard, non-mobile mass on the left shoulder.  
- X-ray: Rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass just above the AC joint, with cranial displacement of humeral head and narrowing of subacromial space.  
- Osseous degenerative changes of humeral head and AC joint.  
- Ultrasound: Supraclavicular fluid collection, anechogenic but heterogeneous, extending from the AC interval; no Doppler signal.  
- Rotator cuff interrogation shows absence of supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head and bony contour irregularity.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Acromioclavicular joint traumatic injury  
   - Pros: Mass near AC joint could suggest hematoma or post-traumatic effusion.  
   - Cons: No history of trauma; clinical presentation is a slowly growing mass; ultrasound shows fluid collection without Doppler signal typical for chronic rather than acute trauma; tendon absence not explained by trauma alone.  

2) Degenerative acromioclavicular joint changes  
   - Pros: Osseous degenerative changes seen on X-ray.  
   - Cons: Does not explain the large fluid collection or soft-tissue mass; tendon loss and large supraclavicular cyst are not typical features of degenerative changes alone.  

3) Acromioclavicular cyst  
   - Pros: Fluid collection adjacent to AC joint; soft-tissue mass is consistent with cyst.  
   - Cons: Does not explain the absence of rotator cuff tendons or humeral head displacement; cyst is likely secondary to another pathology rather than primary.  

4) Soft-tissue tumour  
   - Pros: Rapidly growing, hard, non-mobile mass on clinical exam suggests neoplasm.  
   - Cons: Imaging shows a fluid-filled, anechogenic mass, not solid; no Doppler vascularity; tendon loss and joint space changes suggest secondary to degeneration/injury rather than primary tumour.  

5) Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear  
   - Pros: Classic triad: massive rotator cuff tear (absence of supraspinatus/infraspinatus), AC joint degeneration, and cyst formation proximal to AC joint.  
   - Cons: All imaging and clinical findings match perfectly: fluid-filled cyst, tendon absence, degenerative joint changes, soft-tissue mass. The clinical picture is pathognomonic for this condition.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear (best fits all key findings and is pathognomonic)  
2) Acromioclavicular cyst (close but missing tendon tear and joint degeneration context)  
3) Degenerative acromioclavicular joint changes (limited to bone, does not explain cyst or tendon loss)  
4) Acromioclavicular joint traumatic injury (no trauma history, chronic findings)  
5) Soft-tissue tumour (imaging contradicts soft tissue tumor)  

Final diagnosis: Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear  
</analysis>  

<final>  
Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp (Figure 1).
 
 Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing
 
 a “filarial dance”
 
 sign (Figures 2 and 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp. Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing a “filarial dance” sign.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dirofilariasis, Epidermal inclusion cyst, Cysticercosis, Foreign body granuloma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 71-year-old female with subcutaneous thick-walled cystic lesion in the left temporal scalp region.  
- Ultrasound showed actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures with echogenic walls inside the cyst.  
- "Filarial dance" sign observed on ultrasound.  
- No trauma-related abnormalities on CT.  
- History of recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for 6 months.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Dirofilariasis  
   Pros: Dirofilaria species (canine filaria) can cause subcutaneous nodules with live worms visible on ultrasound. The description of actively mobile tubular serpiginous structures with echogenic walls fits best with live filarial (or dirofilariasis) parasites. The "filarial dance" sign is classic for live filarial worms in tissues.  
   Cons: Usually, dirofilariasis occurs in tropical areas and involves subcutaneous tissues, compatible with this case.

2) Epidermal inclusion cyst  
   Pros: Common cystic lesion of the skin/scalp.  
   Cons: No live mobile organisms would be seen on ultrasound; the "filarial dance" sign is incompatible. So fails.

3) Cysticercosis  
   Pros: Cysticercosis can present as subcutaneous or intramuscular cysts with scolex visible.  
   Cons: The sonographic appearance is typically a cyst with an internal scolex that does not move like serpiginous tubular structures but appears as a dot or echogenic focus; "filarial dance" sign is not seen. Also, lesion location and clinical history (swelling and itching subcutaneous) less typical. So less likely.

4) Foreign body granuloma  
   Pros: Can cause subcutaneous cystic lesions.  
   Cons: No live mobile organisms or "filarial dance" sign would be seen. The ultrasound findings specifically show mobile tubular structures, which do not match foreign body granuloma.

C) Ranking:  
1) Dirofilariasis (best fit with "filarial dance" sonographic sign and mobile tubular structures).  
2) Cysticercosis (some cystic lesions but no live motile worms).  
3) Epidermal inclusion cyst (no worms).  
4) Foreign body granuloma (no worms).

<final> Dirofilariasis </final><|return|>
options: Dirofilariasis | Epidermal inclusion cyst | Cysticercosis | Foreign body granuloma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female presented to the maxillofacial surgeon with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left pre-auricular mass, which she first noticed two years prior. No other symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: An
 
 orthopantomogram
 
 showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion (Figure 1). A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a
 
 dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus
 
 (Figure 2), compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis.
 
 During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table (Figure 3), also consistent with osteomas.
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: An orthopantomogram showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion. A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus, compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis. During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table, also consistent with osteomas.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Cemento-osseous dysplasia, Osteomatosis, Odontomas, Gardner’s syndrome, Hypercementosis, Hereditary multiple exostoses, Fibrous dysplasia
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 18-year-old female with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left preauricular mass.
- Orthopantomogram: large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in left mandibular ramus (ivory-like osteoma), multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions with cortical expansion, persistent primary teeth, caries.
- CT maxillofacial: confirms dense ivory-like exophytic lesion (osteoma) in left mandibular ramus and well-delineated mature bone lesions between tooth roots (hypercementosis).
- Clinical examination also palpated a forehead mass; CT skull showed multiple small sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from outer table (osteomas).
- Overall: multiple osteomas (mandible and skull), hypercementosis, persistent primary teeth.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Cemento-osseous dysplasia:
   - Typically presents in middle-aged African women with mixed radiolucent-radiopaque lesions at tooth apices, not exophytic osteomas.
   - No osteomas or hypercementosis commonly described.
   - CON: Lesion morphology and patient demographics do not match.

2) Osteomatosis:
   - Characterized by multiple osteomas, often in various bones.
   - Could explain multiple osteomas but no mention of other skeletal involvement.
   - Pros: Multiple osteomas present.
   - Cons: Osteomas in head and neck plus hypercementosis and retained primary teeth suggest a syndrome rather than isolated osteomatosis.

3) Odontomas:
   - Usually associated with teeth, appear as mixed radiopaque-radiolucent lesions, and cause delayed tooth eruption.
   - Not exophytic osteomas, no extraoral osteomas.
   - CON: Lesion types and extra-skeletal osteomas not typical.

4) Gardnerâs syndrome:
   - Classical triad: multiple osteomas (including skull and mandible), epidermoid cysts, and colorectal polyposis.
   - Dental anomalies including supernumerary teeth, impacted teeth, and hypercementosis are common.
   - Patient has multiple osteomas (mandible and forehead), hypercementosis, persistent primary teeth.
   - Highly suspect in young patient with these findings.
   - Cons: No mention of cysts or polyps, but syndrome may present incompletely initially.

5) Hypercementosis:
   - Radiopaque cementum-like deposits at tooth roots.
   - Present in this case but is a secondary finding rather than primary syndrome.
   - Does not explain osteomas or mass.

6) Hereditary multiple exostoses:
   - Presence of multiple osteochondromas (cartilage-capped bony projections), usually metaphyseal of long bones.
   - Not typical in jaw or skull bones, and lesions are cartilaginous caps, not ivory-like osteomas.
   - CON: Lesion types and locations are inconsistent.

7) Fibrous dysplasia:
   - Monostotic or polyostotic fibro-osseous lesion, usually ground-glass appearance.
   - Does not produce osteomas or hypercementosis.
   - CON: Findings do not match classic imaging.

C) Ranking:
1) Gardnerâs syndrome - Best fit due to multiple osteomas in jaw and skull, hypercementosis, and retained primary teeth.
2) Osteomatosis - Less likely without other skeletal involvement and dental anomalies.
3) Fibrous dysplasia, cemento-osseous dysplasia, hereditary multiple exostoses, odontomas, hypercementosis - Less consistent with overall clinical and imaging picture.

<final> Gardnerâs syndrome </final><|return|>

 and

 ). Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy. CT was performed to rule out active bleeding and postoperative haematoma.
 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia (Figures 1 and 3). Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged (Figures 2 and 4). There was no involvement of deeper compartments.
 
 No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia. There was no involvement of deeper compartments. No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old male presented with non-bilious emesis and a left thigh cutaneous lesion. The patient was found to have pyloric atresia requiring gastroduodenostomy; this procedure led to intestinal malrotation, and the appendix was removed prophylactically. During surgery, the cutaneous lesion was also biopsied. The patient was transiently jaundiced following surgery; this resolved spontaneously, but a liver ultrasound was requested as part of the workup of this.
 Imaging Findings: Liver US showed a normal biliary tree and 2 hypoechoic liver lesions: the larger a 6 mm lesion in segment IVb (lesion 1). Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) was performed to further characterise. Three lesions were examined with 4 boluses of ultrasound (Total vol. 1.1mL, Optison, GE Healthcare Inc., using a Phillips EPIQ machine). Lesion 1 had increased in size (seg. IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).
 
 To further characterise, a liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast was performed. Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). Lesions 2 (Figure 4d) and 3 were T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to the liver. No lesions showed enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.

A) Key findings:  
- 2-day-old male with pyloric atresia and associated skin lesion (biopsied)

- Two hypoechoic liver lesions on ultrasound, one initially 6 mm in segment IVb which increased to ~34 mm (growth over a short interval)  
- Lesions demonstrated no enhancement on contrast MRI and CEUS, no macroscopic fat, no diffusion restriction  
- Lesion 1 is infiltrative with capsular retraction and rectangular shape, T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense  
- Lesions 2 and 3 are smaller, T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity  
- No diffusion restriction or enhancement argue against high-grade tumors or lymphoma  
- Background: rare congenital condition with pyloric atresia and skin lesion, possibly a genetic syndrome  
- No fat seen within lesions (rules out fat-containing lesions like some hepatoblastomas)  
- Liver MRI showed no enhancement (important: typical hepatoblastomas enhance, lymphomas enhance, neuroblastomas rarely involve liver directly, haemangiomas enhance intensely)  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Hepatoblastoma  
   - Pros: Common liver malignancy in neonates/infants; lesions can be hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2; can be infiltrative with capsular retraction; can enlarge rapidly.  
   - Cons: Typically shows contrast enhancement (mostly hypervascular); usually has detectable AFP elevation; no enhancement here; no macroscopic fat or calcifications; clinical suspicion with pyloric atresia and skin lesion doesn't match.  

2) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Can involve liver and cause hypointense T1 lesions; may cause diffuse infiltration.  
   - Cons: Usually shows diffusion restriction; often enhances after contrast; rare in newborns; no enhancement or diffusion restriction here; patient age and clinical scenario less likely.  

3) Infantile myofibromatosis  
   - Pros: Typically presents with skin/subcutaneous lesions in infants; can have visceral involvement including liver; lesions are hypoechoic on US and can enlarge rapidly; usually no enhancement or only minimal enhancement; no fat; non-malignant but can be aggressive locally.  
   - Cons: Rare liver involvement, but possible; absence of enhancement and diffusion restriction fits; clinical context with skin lesion and pyloric atresia consistent with known association.  

4) Neuroblastoma  
   - Pros: Common abdominal tumor in infants; can metastasize to liver.  
   - Cons: Primary tumor usually adrenal or sympathetic chain, not liver primarily; no primary site observed; liver lesions usually multiple, enhancing; no enhancement here; no typical neuroblastoma imaging features.  

5) Haemangiomata  
   - Pros: Common benign liver tumors in neonates; can be hypoechoic, T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense; show capsular retraction when involuting.  
   - Cons: Usually show characteristic peripheral nodular enhancement with centripetal fill-in on contrast studies; no enhancement here; non-fibrofatty content absent; lesions are usually more rounded, not infiltrative rectangular shape; rapid size increase uncommon without enhancement.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Infantile myofibromatosis (best fit: skin lesion, liver involvement, no enhancement, clinical scenario)  
2) Hepatoblastoma (less likely due to no enhancement, young age, no fat, clinical syndrome mismatch)  
3) Lymphoma (unlikely due to no diffusion restriction or enhancement, very rare in neonates)  
4) Haemangiomata (excluded due to no characteristic enhancement)  
5) Neuroblastoma (least likely, no primary tumor site, lesion characteristics don't fit)  

<final> Infantile myofibromatosis </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman with a known history of retroperitoneal sarcoma, who was receiving radiotherapy, was referred for evaluation of newly palpable lumps in her left breast and left axillary region.
 Imaging Findings: A diagnostic bilateral mammogram (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d; previous mammogram is not available for comparison) shows two circumscribed masses in the left breast upper-outer quadrant.
 
 On the ultrasound of the left breast and axilla (Figures 2a and 2b), there are two adjacent hypo-echoic masses with circumscribed borders in the left breast at 2
 
 o’clock; m
 
 oreover, there is a similar mass in the left axillary region.
 
 The abdominal CT scan with contrast (Figures 3a and 3b) shows a large heterogeneously enhancing mass in the retroperitoneal space with left kidney invasion and aortic wall encasement.
 
 The histopathologic exam reveals highly atypical spindle cells with large pleomorphic hyperchromatic nuclei and high nucleo/cytoplasmic ratio (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.

 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres (Figures 1a and 1b) to around 22x15 mm (Figures 2a and 2b), and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures (Figures 3 and 4), with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).

- Lesions demonstrate feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins.  
- Multiple bilateral lesions with vascular enhancement and feeding/draining vessels.  
- The patient is being staged for cancer, and general symptoms are interpreted within oncological context.

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons:  
1) Hypervascular lung metastases:  
- Pros: Occurs in the setting of known malignancy; hypervascular metastases can show arterial phase enhancement; peripheral nodules are typical for metastases.  
- Cons: Metastases usually do not demonstrate feeding/draining vessels from lobar arteries and veins; they often appear solid/tissue density rather than vascular structures; vascular architecture resembling arteries and veins with direct feeding/draining is not typical for metastases.  
  
2) Pulmonary sequestration:  
- Pros: Sequestration includes systemic feeding vessels, usually from the aorta, not lobar arteries. Not typical bilateral or multiple small peripheral lesions.  
- Cons: Usually a single lesion; feeding vessels from systemic circulation (aorta), not lobar pulmonary arteries; rarely bilateral. 

3) Bronchocoele:  
- Pros: Can appear as low-density structures, sometimes nodular.  
- Cons: Typically doesn't enhance like vascular structures; no feeding or drainage vessels; not hypervascular; not typically multiple bilateral peripheral nodular lesions. 

4) Pulmonary varix:  
- Pros: Varices are dilated pulmonary veins that can enhance with contrast.  
- Cons: Usually localized, not multiple bilateral nodules; varices enlarge veins, not creating nodules with arterial feeding vessels; no feeding arterial vessels.  

5) Pulmonary arterial aneurysm: 
- Pros: Vascular lesions; can enhance with arterial phase.  
- Cons: Rarely multiple bilateral peripheral nodules; usually larger focal areas; does not show feeding/draining vessels from lobar arteries and veins in the pattern described; usually singular or few.  

6) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations (PAVMs):  
- Pros: Classic findings include nodular or vascular lesions enhancing similarly to pulmonary arteries; feeding vessel from pulmonary artery and draining pulmonary vein; often multiple, bilateral, peripheral; enhancement in arterial phase; range of sizes; multiple images consistent with AVMs.  
- Cons: None significant; fits well.  

7) Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications:  
- Pros: Some systemic supply to lungs, but usually systemic arteries (e.g., from aorta).  
- Cons: Typically singular or few; doesn't match multiple bilateral distal nodules; feeding from lobar arteries (pulmonary arteries) excludes systemic aberrant vessels.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations â meets all criteria: multiple bilateral peripheral nodules, arterial phase enhancement similar to vessels, feeding arteries from lobar pulmonary arteries, drainage into lobar veins, size variation.  
2) Hypervascular lung metastases â plausible due to malignancy but lacks vascular structure with feeding/draining vessels.  
3) Others less likely based on location, number, vascular supply/drainage patterns.  

Thus, the most pathognomonic and fitting diagnosis is Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations.  
</analysis>  
<final>Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture, and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT. Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain. The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Calcific tendinitis, Neurogenic heterotopic ossification, Myositis ossificans, Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease, Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva, Tumoral calcinosis, Sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 39-year-old man with traumatic tibial and fibular fractures and TBI from a motor vehicle accident.
- Imaging after trauma showed comminuted tibial fracture, overlapping fibular fracture, and multifocal subarachnoid hemorrhage.
- Six months later, while bedridden and without new trauma, shoulder pain led to imaging showing extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, with mature ossification.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1. Calcific tendinitis
   - Pros: Can cause calcification around joints.
   - Cons: Usually presents as focal calcific deposits in tendons, not extensive mature heterotopic ossification, and does not relate to trauma or TBI. Bilateral extensive ossifications unlikely.

2. Neurogenic heterotopic ossification
   - Pros: Classic after TBI or central nervous system injury, especially with prolonged immobility. Heterotopic mature bone formation around joints, especially hips and shoulders. This case fits perfectly: TBI, bedridden status, and mature bilateral heterotopic ossification in shoulders.
   - Cons: None apparent.

3. Myositis ossificans
   - Pros: Ossification within muscles after trauma, often focal.
   - Cons: Typically localized to a single muscle, related to direct muscle trauma, not bilateral extensive joint-related ossification months after CNS injury. Also, myositis ossificans shows zoning pattern, not mature joint heterotopic ossification.

4. Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease
   - Pros: Can cause calcifications or ossifications.
   - Cons: Usually systemic and not localized to trauma or TBI sites; unlikely to cause mature ossification in glenohumeral joints post trauma.

5. Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva
   - Pros: Progressive heterotopic ossification.
   - Cons: Rare genetic disease with episodic flare-ups; usually starts in childhood, not triggered by trauma or TBI in adults; bilateral shoulder involvement possible but unlikely in this acute traumatic context.

6. Tumoral calcinosis
   - Pros: Calcium phosphate deposits in soft tissues.
   - Cons: Deposits are not mature heterotopic bone, no relation to trauma or TBI, usually periarticular but not mature bone formation.

7. Sarcoma
   - Pros: Can involve ossification.
   - Cons: Rarely presents with bilateral mature heterotopic ossifications after trauma and TBI; clinical context and imaging do not suggest malignancy.

C) Ranking:
1. Neurogenic heterotopic ossification (best match: TBI, prolonged immobility, mature bilateral joint ossification)
2. Myositis ossificans (less likely due to distribution and timeline)
3. Calcific tendinitis (unlikely due to distribution and maturity of ossification)
4. Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva (clinical context less typical)
5. Tumoral calcinosis (does not fit mature bone formation context)
6. Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease (does not fit trauma-related ossification)
7. Sarcoma (least likely given clinical and imaging context)
</analysis>

<final>Neurogenic heterotopic ossification</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 81-year-old woman who was presumed to be suffering from polymyalgia rheumatica and was receiving 2.5mg/week of ledertrexate. The patient suffered from chondrocalcinosis of the wrist joints, bilateral arthritis in her hands and repeated bouts of plurifocal synovitis in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. The patient presented for an ultrasound due to Achilles tendon pain due to suspected bilateral Achilles tendonitis.
 Imaging Findings: Multiple hyperechogenic elongated deposits with acoustic shadowing are located within the central part of the right Achilles tendon and following its long axis (Figures 1 and 2), as well as in the left Achilles tendon (Figures 3 and 4). The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.

   - Pros: could explain calcifications; trauma can cause calcific deposits.  
   - Cons: No history of trauma mentioned; bilateral symmetrical tendons involved; patient has chronic crystal arthropathy; trauma-related calcifications are usually isolated, unilateral or focal, not symmetrical.  
   - Verdict: Does not fit context; unlikely.  

2) Gout related-intra-tendinous calcifications  
   - Pros: gout can cause tendon involvement with tophi and calcifications; can cause hypoechogenic thickening.  
   - Cons: Gout deposits usually show different sonographic appearance (more irregular, heterogeneous, with the double contour sign on joint surfaces, not classic acoustic shadowing); patient has no mention of hyperuricemia or gout; chondrocalcinosis and polymyalgia rheumatica more consistent with CPPD.  
   - Verdict: Less likely given clinical context and imaging characteristics.  

3) Idiopathic intra-tendinous calcifications  
   - Pros: idiopathic calcifications occur without systemic disease.  
   - Cons: patient has systemic crystal arthropathy (chondrocalcinosis), making idiopathic less plausible; bilateral symmetrical involvement points to systemic cause.  
   - Verdict: Unlikely in presence of known crystal arthropathy.  

4) Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease  
   - Pros: Patient has known chondrocalcinosis (CPPD) in wrist joints, which is pathognomonic for CPPD.  
   - Bilateral symmetrical tendon involvement with hyperechogenic linear deposits with acoustic shadowing is typical for CPPD tendon involvement.  
   - Achilles tendon involvement in CPPD is known but rare; however, patient history supports systemic CPPD.  
   - Cons: Achilles tendon involvement less common but possible; sonographic features match.  
   - Verdict: Strong candidate given clinical history and imaging.  

5) Hydroxyapatite deposits  
   - Pros: Hydroxyapatite crystals cause calcific tendinopathy; can produce hyperechoic deposits with acoustic shadowing; often affects rotator cuff and other tendons.  
   - Cons: Hydroxyapatite deposition is uncommon in the Achilles tendon; the pattern is usually focal, not multiple elongated deposits along the entire tendon; no mention of hydroxyapatite arthropathy or related conditions; patient has CPPD which better explains chondrocalcinosis.  
   - Verdict: Less likely compared to CPPD.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease  
2) Gout related-intra-tendinous calcifications  
3) Hydroxyapatite deposits  
4) Idiopathic intra-tendinous calcifications  
5) Intra-tendinous calcifications following trauma  

Tie-break: Acoustic shadowing and clinical history of chondrocalcinosis strongly favor CPPD. Patient's age and systemic symptoms also support this.  

</analysis>  
<final>Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old athlete with chest discomfort and palpitations presented to the cardiologist. Electrocardiogram showed low-density ventricular extrasystoles at rest, which increased in density with stress. Echocardiography revealed mild mitral regurgitation and mild left ventricular hypertrophy
 
 with a basal septal thickness of 12 mm.
 Imaging Findings: Cardiac magnetic resonance was performed on a 3 Tesla MRI scanner. Steady-state free precession (SSFP) sequences identify billowing of both mitral leaflets, with mitral valve prolapse (MVP) of the posterior leaflet (5 mm), mitral annular disjunction (6 mm) and “curling” (Figures 1a and 1b; yellow arrow). Mitral regurgitation was not significant. Biventricular function was normal.
 
 Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) was present in the subendocardial–intramyocardial layer of the basal inferolateral wall, suggesting replacement fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; white arrows). Native T1 mapping in the same region was elevated: 1360 ms (local normal range 1150–1250 ms), confirming myocardial fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; yellow arrow). Cardiac CT ruled out significant stenosis in the circumflex artery and posterolateral artery (Figure 3; circumflex artery: yellow arrow).
PostDescription:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 4-month-old male infant presented with a palpable mass on his arm. The parents noticed it since he was a month and a half old. Clinical examination showed a painless mass without signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (US), the mass was poorly defined and hyperechoic compared to muscle, located in the subcutaneous tissue. The lesion exhibited a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands with a hyperechoic border [1] (Figure 1a).
 
 It showed low vascularity on colour Doppler (Figure 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the arm, resting on the fascia without involving the underlying muscle. T1 and T1 fat-saturated images showed isointense tissue relative to muscle, with interspersed fatty foci (Figures 2a and 2b). No enhancement was noted after gadolinium injection (Figure 2c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydro-nephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy
 
 procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa
 
 , t
 
 he patient was supine.
 
 The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a
 
 tortuosity of the ureter
 
 , causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder.
 
 Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised
 
 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis.
 
 The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.

The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa, the patient was supine. The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a tortuosity of the ureter, causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder.    - Pros: Post-transplant vascular interventions are common.

   - Pros: Similar reasoning as artery stent.  
   - Cons: Vein stent typically would cause venous congestion or thrombosis, not ureteral stricture or hydronephrosis; findings do not suggest vein pathology.  

3) Post-transplant ureteral stent removal

4) Post-transplant ureteral stent placement  
   - Pros: Ureteral stents are placed to manage or prevent ureteral strictures post-transplant.

3) Post-transplant renal artery stent (unlikely given findings).  
4) Post-transplant renal vein stenting (least likely).  
</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old lady presented to the emergency department with accelerated hypertension. She had a history of recurrent episodes of sudden onset palpitations, headache and giddiness for the past two years, with uncontrolled hypertension despite being on multiple antihypertensive medications. Her blood investigations revealed elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL).
 Imaging Findings: Trans-abdominal ultrasound (Figures 1a and 1b) showed a well-defined heterogeneously hyperechoic lesion in the right adnexa, seen separate from the right ovary, measuring approximately 4.7 x 5 x 4.8 cm, and with high internal vascularity.
 
 CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, and 3b) showed a hyperdense mass (average HU of 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and few central non-enhancing areas suggestive of necrosis. It was located in the right adnexa and paracervical region, abutting the uterus (which was displaced to the left). It was abutting the posterior urinary bladder wall anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly with no invasion of the right ureter, iliac vessels and lateral pelvic side wall.
 
 On MRI of the pelvis, the lesion was deep to the lateral pelvic fascia on the right, heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1WI (Figures 4b and 5), heterogeneously hyperintense on T2WI (
 
 “light bulb sign”
 
 ) (Figure 4a), with areas of restricted diffusion on DWI and maintained fat planes with the adjacent structures (Figures 6a and 6b).
 
 On 68Ga-DOTATATE scan, there was intense homogeneous SSTR (somatostatin receptor) expression in a large well-defined soft tissue density mass in the right adnexa and paracervical region with maximum standardised uptake value (SUV max) of 20.19 (Figure 7).

A) Key findings:  
- Young adult female with recurrent episodes of palpitations, headache, giddiness, and uncontrolled hypertension.

- Imaging shows a well-defined, heterogeneously hyperechoic hypervascular lesion in the right adnexa, separate from the right ovary.  
- CT reveals a hyperdense mass (average HU 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and central necrosis.  
- MRI shows a heterogeneously iso- to hyperintense T1 and T2 lesion with "light bulb sign" hyperintensity, restricted diffusion in parts, and well-maintained fat planes.
