- MRI: Encapsulated lesion with high T1 signal (~subcutaneous fat), heterogeneously high T2 signal, partial fat suppression, homogeneous hypervascular enhancement with curvilinear branching septations, no diffusion restriction.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Lipoma
   - Pros: Contains fat, well-defined capsule, non-enhancing or minimally enhancing.
   - Cons: This lesion shows significant soft tissue, heterogeneous enhancement, prominent vessels, and hypervascular septations which are atypical for a simple lipoma. Also, complex and vascular lesion less typical.

2) Liposarcoma
   - Pros: Complex soft tissue with fat, heterogeneous enhancement.
   - Cons: Usually shows restricted diffusion, invasive or infiltrative margins, often unencapsulated or irregular. This mass is encapsulated and shows no diffusion restriction. Also, prominent internal vessels and hypervascular branching septations are less common. Location (iliopsoas) possible but less typical. Imaging did not show aggressive features. 

3) Angiomyolipoma
   - Pros: Contains fat, soft tissue, and vascular components; hypervascular enhancement with branching vessels; encapsulated; common in kidney but can be extrarenal, including psoas muscles. MRI signal high T1, heterogeneous T2, partial fat suppression. No diffusion restriction. 
   - Cons: More commonly renal but extrarenal locations known. Clinical context and imaging consistent with this.

4) Hibernoma
   - Pros: Contains fat, often in soft tissues.
   - Cons: Typically no prominent vascularity or heterogeneous enhancement; no significant soft tissue or vessels; usually homogeneously fatty with slight T1/T2 differences but not hypervascular. Less likely to have branching vessels or enhancement characteristics described.

5) Myelolipoma
   - Pros: Contains fat and hematopoietic tissue, often in adrenal glands.
   - Cons: Very uncommon in iliopsoas or psoas muscles; no prominent vascularity or hypervascular enhancement described; usually well-defined but not hypervascular; not typical location.

6) Fat necrosis
   - Pros: Contains fat, soft tissue components.
   - Cons: Usually irregular margins, no prominent internal vessels or hypervascular septations; enhancement may be peripheral or irregular but not organized hypervascular branching septa; clinical history often trauma or inflammation; this lesion is encapsulated and organized, less likely fat necrosis.

C) Ranking:
1) Angiomyolipoma - best matches complex fat-soft tissue mass with prominent vessels and hypervascular branching septal enhancement, encapsulated, no diffusion restriction.
2) Liposarcoma - somewhat fits fat-soft tissue with heterogeneous enhancement but lacks invasive features and diffusion restriction.
3) Myelolipoma - composition matches but unusual site and no hypervascularity.
4) Lipoma - too simple and no prominent vascular or soft tissue components.
5) Hibernoma - lacks prominent vascularity and enhancement pattern.
6) Fat necrosis - morphology and vascular pattern less consistent.

Thus, Angiomyolipoma is the most appropriate diagnosis.

</analysis><final>Angiomyolipoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 23-year-old woman presented with two weeks of abdominal distension, pelvic pain and urinary frequency. Emergency thoraco-abdominal computed tomography (CT) was performed, followed shortly by dedicated pelvic MRI for loco-regional characterisation.
 Imaging Findings: Initial contrast-enhanced pelvic CT (Figures 1a and 1b) demonstrated a 17 × 16 × 12 cm, predominantly solid, left retro-uterine mass with heterogeneous enhancement on the left ovary, anterior displacement of the uterus, and compression of both distal ureters, resulting in moderate bilateral hydroureteronephrosis. No macroscopic fat, calcification or pathological lymph-node enlargement was identified.
 
 Subsequent pelvic MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) showed mainly solid tissue with intermediate T2 signal (Figures 2a and 2b) and avid post-gadolinium enhancement (Figure 3), containing scattered cystic-necrotic foci. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) showed marked hyperintensity of the solid components with low ADC values, consistent with high cellularity (Figures 4a and 4b). The tumour–uterus interface was smooth, and the right ovary appeared normal, displaced anteriorly. Ascites was minimal and confined to the pouch of Douglas.
 
 No peritoneal implants or pathological lymphadenopathy were identified on either CT or MRI.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A previously healthy 3-year-old boy presented with recurrent atraumatic pain and refusal to walk for several weeks. The right proximal lower leg was swollen, and mobilisation was painful. Laboratory analysis at admission showed increased white blood cell count of 12.6 x 10³/mm³ and C-reactive protein of 20.2 mg/l.
 Imaging Findings: Radiography showed an irregularly moth-eaten mixed osteolytic-sclerotic lesion with a wide zone of transition in the proximal tibial diaphysis. The cortex was permeated with a transverse pathological fracture, interrupted lamellar periosteal reaction, and soft tissue swelling (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed extensive bone marrow replacement in the proximal tibia with adjacent soft tissue mass showing heterogeneous contrast enhancement, cortical permeation, bone destruction with focal cortical breakthrough (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d). Bone marrow changes were hypointense on T1-weighted images (WI) and heterogeneous hyperintense on STIR and fat-suppressed PD images.
 
 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography–computed tomography (FDG PET–CT) scan showed markedly increased uptake in the proximal right tibia, adjacent soft tissue, and right popliteal, inguinal, iliac and retroperitoneal lymph nodes (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Other distant metastases were absent.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 66-year-old woman developed progressive neck pain, headache, and ascending lower-limb paraesthesia over 3 months that culminated in sudden bilateral leg weakness and ataxia. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) showed lymphocytic pleocytosis with hyperproteinorrachia, and a 2024 mediastinal node biopsy revealed non-necrotising granulomas. Her history includes uterine ablation (2006) and sinus surgery (1995).
 Imaging Findings: Sagittal T2-weighted MRI of the spine (Figure 1) revealed diffuse hyperintensity and swelling from C2 to T10 with a sharp transition at T11. Post-gadolinium T1 images (Figure 2) showed confluent dorsal subpial and central canal enhancement, producing the axial “trident sign”. There was patchy nodular enhancement involving the posterior and circumferential pial surfaces. The conus was spared.
 
 Brain MRI demonstrated two punctate cortical–subcortical enhancing foci without mass effect (Figures 3a and 3b). CT chest (Figures 4a and 4b) confirmed bulky right paratracheal and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Compared with prior studies (December 2020), cord swelling and enhancement were mildly reduced after steroid therapy.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Clinical: 66-year-old woman with progressive neck pain, headache, ascending lower-limb paresthesia over 3 months, then sudden bilateral leg weakness and ataxia.  
- CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis with hyperproteinorrachia (indicative of inflammatory/infectious process).  
- Pathology: mediastinal node biopsy shows non-necrotising granulomas (consistent with sarcoidosis).  
- MRI spine: diffuse T2 hyperintensity and swelling from C2 to T10, sharp stop at T11 (segmental involvement with conus spared).  
- Post-gadolinium T1: confluent dorsal subpial and central canal enhancement creating the axial "trident sign". Patchy nodular enhancement of posterior and circumferential pial surfaces. Conus spared.  
- Brain MRI: two punctate cortical-subcortical enhancing foci without mass effect (consistent with neurosarcoidosis lesions).  
- CT chest: bulky right paratracheal and mediastinal lymphadenopathy (consistent with systemic sarcoidosis).  
- Response: mild reduction of cord swelling and enhancement after steroids is supportive of an inflammatory process like sarcoidosis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Spinal neurosarcoidosis with trident sign  
- Pros: Clinical systemic sarcoidosis (mediastinal lymphadenopathy with granulomas), typical MRI spinal findings including "trident sign", CSF lymphocytosis, and good response to steroids.  
- Cons: None significant; matches well.  

2) Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD)  
- Pros: Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis can affect long segments.  
- Cons: No mention of anti-AQP4 antibodies, no clinical features like optic neuritis, and imaging lacks typical features. Also, "trident sign" is not a classic feature. CSF usually less pleocytosis. No lymphadenopathy or granulomas.  

3) MOG antibody-associated myelitis  
- Pros: Can cause longitudinally extensive myelitis.  
- Cons: No mention of MOG antibodies, no typical MRI features like fluffy lesions or brain lesions typical for MOG. No systemic sarcoidosis features.  

4) Intramedullary metastasis  
- Pros: Could enhance and cause cord signal changes.  
- Cons: Usually focal lesions rather than diffuse swelling over many segments, no primary tumor given, systemic sarcoidosis present, and "trident sign" atypical for metastasis.  

5) Infectious granulomatous myelitis  
- Pros: Granulomatous inflammation and cord enhancement possible in infections like tuberculosis or fungal.  
- Cons: No necrotising granulomas (non-necrotising granulomas more typical of sarcoidosis), no systemic infection signs, no history of infection, CSF lymphocytic but no mention of infection markers.  

6) Primary intramedullary lymphoma  
- Pros: Enhancement and cord enlargement possible.  
- Cons: Rare, usually focal lesions, no systemic sarcoidosis, no lymphadenopathy. Unlikely with diffuse long segment involvement and "trident sign".  

C) Ranking:  
1) Spinal neurosarcoidosis with trident sign (best match to imaging, pathology, systemic sarcoidosis)  
2) NMOSD (possible but less specific features)  
3) MOG antibody myelitis (possible but less likely here)  
4) Infectious granulomatous myelitis (possible but no necrosis or infection evidence)  
5) Intramedullary metastasis (unlikely due to diffuse pattern)  
6) Primary intramedullary lymphoma (least likely)

</analysis>  
<final> Spinal neurosarcoidosis with trident sign </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old patient with a history of type 2 diabetes, arterial hypertension, and knee osteoarthritis treated with intra-articular steroids was admitted due to fever of unknown origin. The patient reported lumbar pain for the past week, with no associated symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast and contrast-enhanced CT of the skull, thorax and abdomen was performed to identify a possible infectious focus. The key findings included heterogeneous density in both psoas muscles due to the presence of gas bubbles, with no collections detected following contrast administration (Figures 1a and 1b). In addition to the gas identified in the psoas muscles, gas bubbles were observed within the L3 vertebral body.
 
 Unenhanced and contrast-enhanced lumbar spine MRI was performed. The axial T2-weighted image (Figure 2a) shows an enlarged left psoas muscle due to multiple round collections with increased signal intensity. A similar collection is observed in the ipsilateral paraspinal muscle and the right psoas muscle. In the axial post-contrast T1-weighted sequence (Figure 2b), the described lesions, which exhibit enhancement after contrast administration, are also seen (marked with blue stars). Sagittal T1-weighted images pre (Figure 2c) and post-contrast (Figure 2d) demonstrate heterogeneous very subtle enhancement of the L2, L3, L4, and L5 vertebral bodies, as well as the intervertebral discs. The sagittal views demonstrate lesions in the psoas and paraspinal muscles, with hyperintense signal on T2-weighted (Figure 2e) and STIR (Figure 2f) sequences at the level of the L2, L3, L4, and L5 vertebral bodies.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient with risk factors: diabetes, hypertension, intra-articular steroid injections (immunosuppression).  
- Presentation: fever of unknown origin, lumbar pain lasting a week.  
- Imaging: CT shows gas bubbles in both psoas muscles and L3 vertebral body; no collections on contrast CT.  
- MRI: multiple round collections in psoas and paraspinal muscles with high signal on T2/STIR (suggestive of abscesses); enhancement after contrast administration; L2-L5 vertebral bodies and intervertebral discs show subtle heterogeneous enhancement; vertebral bodies and discs are involved.  
- Gas within vertebral body and muscles strongly suggests gas-forming bacterial infection.  
- MRI shows multi-level involvement (L2-L5) with collections.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Tuberculous spondylodiscitis  
   Pros: Can involve multiple vertebral bodies and discs; associated with paraspinal abscesses.  
   Cons: Typically does not produce gas bubbles; slower onset; often no fever of unknown origin with gas bubbles; risk factors less typical; psoas abscesses rare and usually cold abscesses without gas.  
2) Pyogenic spondylodiscitis  
   Pros: Can involve multiple vertebral bodies and discs; common in diabetics and immunosuppressed; gas formation common due to gas-producing bacteria; psoas muscle abscesses common; fever is typical.  
   Cons: None significant.  
3) Charcot joint  
   Pros: Can involve degenerative changes with inflammation.  
   Cons: No relation to vertebral infection or psoas abscess; no gas formation; clinical scenario inconsistent.  
4) Modic type 1 endplate changes  
   Pros: Involve endplates with inflammation and edema.  
   Cons: No abscess formation or gas; no vertebral or disc destruction or collections; clinical context incorrect.  
5) Schmorl’s node  
   Pros: Endplate herniation into vertebral body.  
   Cons: No infection, no abscess, no gas; unrelated.  
6) Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH)  
   Pros: Can affect vertebrae in adults.  
   Cons: No abscess, no collections, no gas, no fever; clinical presentation inconsistent.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Pyogenic spondylodiscitis: matches all clinical and imaging findings (gas, abscesses, multi-level vertebral and disc involvement, fever in diabetic/immunocompromised patient).  
2) Tuberculous spondylodiscitis: similar vertebral/disc involvement but lacks gas and typical abscess characteristics; slower onset less compatible.  
3) Others are not compatible with infection or imaging findings.  
Thus, pyogenic spondylodiscitis best fits.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Pyogenic spondylodiscitis

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with long-standing recurrent epistaxis and progressive fatigue. Physical examination revealed mucocutaneous telangiectasias on the nasal mucosa and tongue, as well as mild hepatomegaly. Laboratory tests showed iron-deficiency anaemia and mildly altered liver function tests, with cholestasis but no hyperbilirubinemia.
 Imaging Findings: Colour-Doppler abdominal ultrasound revealed multiple dilated arterial vessels in both liver lobes, corresponding to branches of the hepatic artery, with one vessel forming a direct communication with the main portal vein (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Subsequent contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated diffuse dilatation of intrahepatic arterial branches and early filling of the dilated hepatic veins during arterial phase, suggestive of arteriovenous shunting, and multiple small hypervascular nodules consistent with hepatic telangiectasias. Mild intrahepatic biliary duct dilatation was also noted, likely due to extrinsic compression by dilated vessels (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Further evaluation with MRI confirmed the presence of intrahepatic arteriovenous malformations. Post-contrast T1-weighted Dixon sequences and MR angiography provided superior characterisation of the vascular anomalies, ruling out portal vein thrombosis and parenchymal changes typical of chronic liver disease (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old patient undergoes a scheduled MRI study due to a one-year history of migraine-like headaches, accompanied by visual disturbances, paraesthesias in the right upper limb, and instability before and during the pain.
 Imaging Findings: The MRI reveals a lesion located in the right temporal horn of the lateral ventricle, with a prominent cystic component and a solid nodule that appears hypointense on T1-weighted images (Figures 1a and 1b) and hyperintense on T2-weighted images (Figure 2). This solid, heterogeneous nodule shows intense enhancement following gadolinium administration (Figures 1c, 1d, and 1e) and areas of microcystic architecture, without evidence of subependymal spread. Extensive T2-FLAIR hyperintensity is observed throughout the surrounding white matter, consistent with vasogenic oedema. The mass exerts a moderate compressive effect on the ventricular system, causing a slight midline shift (Figure 2).
 
 Notably, the lesion displays prominent draining veins, reflecting its hypervascular nature (Figures 3a and 3b). No diffusion restriction is identified on DWI sequences (Figure 4). Perfusion imaging confirms a highly vascularised tumour (Figure 5a, 5b, 5c).
 
 Magnetic resonance spectroscopy demonstrates a prominent lipid and lactate peak, likely indicative of necrotic components (Figure 6).

A) Key findings:

- Location of lesion: right temporal horn of the lateral ventricle  
- Lesion characteristics: prominent cystic component with a solid, heterogeneous, intensely enhancing nodule on gadolinium post-contrast  
- Signal intensities: solid nodule hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2  
- No subependymal spread  
- Surrounding vasogenic oedema (extensive T2-FLAIR hyperintensity)  
- Moderate compressive effect and slight midline shift  
- Prominent draining veins indicating hypervascular nature  
- No diffusion restriction on DWI (excludes high cellularity lesions like lymphoma or abscess)  
- Perfusion: highly vascularized tumor  
- MR spectroscopy: prominent lipid and lactate peaks suggesting necrotic components  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Haemangioblastoma  
   - Pros: Hypervascular tumor, common in cerebellum but can rarely occur supratentorially; cystic with mural nodule; intense enhancement; prominent draining vessels; MR spectroscopy lipid/lactate peaks consistent with necrosis.  
   - Cons: Most are in cerebellum or spinal cord; supratentorial location less common; patient's age is compatible but location is atypical.  

2) Astrocytoma  
   - Pros: Can be cystic with mural nodule; can cause edema and mass effect; relatively common; enhancement variable.  
   - Cons: Usually less intensely enhancing mural nodule; typically not as hypervascular with prominent draining veins; MR spectroscopy often shows elevated choline rather than lipid/lactate necrosis peaks; absence of diffusion restriction less specific.  

3) Medulloblastoma  
   - Pros: Hypervascular, solid, in children mostly; can involve fourth ventricle or cerebellum.  
   - Cons: Age is atypical (mostly pediatric); location in lateral ventricle is not typical; usually solid and highly cellular with diffusion restriction; no cystic component with mural nodule typical for medulloblastoma.  

4) Meningioma  
   - Pros: Can be extra-axial with cystic changes; intense homogeneous enhancement; hypervascular; often attached to dura.  
   - Cons: Usually in convexities, falx, sphenoid wing; rare in ventricular system; typically does not have cystic lesion with mural nodule in ventricles; absence of dural tail; lipid/lactate peaks not typical.  

5) Metastasis  
   - Pros: Hypervascular; can show cystic and solid components; can have necrotic areas with lipid/lactate peaks; can cause vasogenic edema.  
   - Cons: Patient has no known primary cancer history; solitary lesion in lateral ventricle less common; usually multiple lesions; age and clinical scenario not typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Haemangioblastoma: fits classic cystic with enhancing mural nodule, hypervascularity with prominent draining veins, lipid/lactate peaks (necrosis), though location is supratentorial but known rare site.  
2) Astrocytoma: less vascular enhancement pattern, no dural attachment, but cystic and solid possible; necrosis can occur but less hypervascular.  
3) Metastasis: possible with necrosis and hypervascularity but less likely given solitary lesion and no known primary.  
4) Meningioma: less likely given intraventricular location and absence of dural tail.  
5) Medulloblastoma: unlikely due to age and location.  

Therefore, the most fitting diagnosis is Haemangioblastoma.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Haemangioblastoma  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old lady presented with a gradually progressive, painless, cystic swelling in the left infra-auricular region for the last 12 months. No other swellings were noted in the neck. There was no prior history of ear discharge or parotitis. Clinical examination showed no evidence of skin changes or facial nerve palsy.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography reveals a cystic lesion with internal echoes within the substance of the left parotid gland with a beak-like projection on its deeper aspect (Figure 1).
 
 CT scan demonstrates a well-defined, homogeneous, smoothly marginated, non-enhancing lesion (density of 35 HU before and 36 HU after contrast administration) located in the left parotid gland, causing thinning and outward displacement of the residual gland parenchyma (Figure 2).
 
 MRI reveals a T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, and STIR hyperintense, intra-parotid lesion occupying superficial and deep lobes of the parotid with a small blind-ending tract projecting anteromedially into the parapharyngeal space. No fistulous tracts to the external auditory canal were noted. There was no evidence of fat stranding or enlarged lymph nodes (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

A) Key findings:  
- 42-year-old female with a painless, slowly progressive cystic swelling in the left infra-auricular region.  
- Ultrasonography shows cystic lesion with internal echoes within the substance of the left parotid gland, with a beak-like projection on its deeper aspect.  
- CT shows well-defined, homogeneous, smoothly marginated, non-enhancing lesion (density ~35 HU both pre- and post-contrast), causing thinning and outward displacement of parotid parenchyma.  
- MRI demonstrates a T1 hypointense, T2 and STIR hyperintense intra-parotid lesion occupying both superficial and deep lobes, with a small blind-ending tract projecting anteromedially into the parapharyngeal space.  
- No skin changes, facial nerve palsy, fistulous tracts to external auditory canal, fat stranding, or lymphadenopathy.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) First branchial cleft cyst  
   - Pros: Typically cystic lesion near the parotid gland or external auditory canal; often have tract/cyst extension into parapharyngeal space consistent with blind-ending tract; typically present as cystic mass.  
   - Cons: Usually located near or involving the external auditory canal or adjacent to parotid gland, often superficial; rare for the lesion to be entirely intra-parotid and cause displacement of parotid parenchyma from within; no fistulous tract to external auditory canal noted here.  

2) Lymphepithelial cyst  
   - Pros: Cystic lesion within parotid gland, typical internal cystic nature; may cause parotid gland displacement; T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense consistent.  
   - Cons: Usually well encapsulated with no tract or extension into parapharyngeal space noted; no beak-like projection with tract described.  

3) Warthinâs tumour  
   - Pros: Occurs in parotid gland, usually cystic component, common in 5th-6th decades; T2 hyperintense cystic areas possible.  
   - Cons: Typically enhancing lesion with solid and cystic components, here there is no enhancement; also, Warthinâs tumors are solid-cystic rather than purely cystic; no description of solid components or characteristic bilateral or multifocal occurrence.  

4) Sialocele  
   - Pros: Cystic lesion within or related to parotid gland, often non-enhancing; may cause fluid collection adjacent to parotid.  
   - Cons: Usually related to trauma or ductal injury; no history of prior trauma; no ductal connection or fistula; usually not intra-parotid occupying both lobes; no tract to parapharyngeal space.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) First branchial cleft cyst (the blind-ending tract projecting into parapharyngeal space and being intra-parotid is most suggestive of developmental cyst with tract, typical for branchial cleft cysts).  
2) Lymphepithelial cyst (no tract or extension noted).  
3) Warthinâs tumour (lack of enhancement and purely cystic nature make it less likely).  
4) Sialocele (no trauma or ductal injury history, and the extent of lesion not typical).  

The presence of a blind-ending tract projecting into the parapharyngeal space is a key pathognomonic feature favoring a branchial cleft cyst, especially a first branchial cleft cyst when related to the parotid gland. This tract also correlates with the deep beak-like projection seen on ultrasonography. Therefore, the most probable diagnosis is the first branchial cleft cyst.  
</analysis>  
<final>First branchial cleft cyst</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old male presented with complaints of headaches for 6 months. No history of loss of consciousness, projectile vomiting or seizures. No history of trauma. The patient is a known case of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension on treatment for 10 years. On examination, macrognathia and frontal bossing were noted.
 Imaging Findings: The skull radiograph (Figure 1a) shows an enlarged sella turcica, frontal sinus hyperpneumatisation, and calvarial hyperostosis. Prognathism and widened mandibular angle are seen (Figure 1b). Hand and feet radiographs (Figures 2a and 2b) show spade-like distal phalanges with prominent ungual tufts. The chest radiograph (Figure 3) reveals widened glenohumeral joints and thickened scapular borders.
 
 CT brain shows calvarial hyperostosis (Figure 4a) and a hyperdense sellar mass (Figure 4b).
 
 MRI reveals a lobulated sellar-suprasellar lesion—T1 isointense (Figure 7), T2 and FLAIR hyperintense with “snowman” appearance—causing sella widening and displacing adjacent vessels (Figures 5a, 5b, and 6), with heterogeneous enhancement (Figures 9b and 9c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 61-year-old male, with a history of a pontine stroke 10 weeks before, presented with new neurological symptoms of staccato speech and an uncoordinated gait. Neurological examination revealed nystagmus, mild dysarthria, truncal and limb ataxia, and an unsteady gait. An MRI was performed to rule out a new ischemic stroke.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI showed a cavitated lesion in the left paramedian pons, hypointense on T1-weighted images (WI) (Figure 1a), hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 1b), without diffusion restriction (Figure 1c), consistent with the previous pontine stroke. Additionally, symmetrical ovoid lesions in the middle cerebellar peduncles (MCPs), hypointense on T1WI (Figure 1d) and hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 1e), and mild T2WI hyperintensity in the left anterior pyramid (Figure 1g), without diffusion restriction (Figures 1f and 1h), were also noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old man presented to the Emergency Department with a 4-day history of right iliac fossa pain. He denied fever, nausea, or vomiting. Physical examination revealed rebound tenderness. Laboratory tests showed elevated C-reactive protein (19.6 mg/L) with a normal white-cell count. Two months earlier, he had undergone a laparoscopic appendectomy.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (Figure 1) revealed a blind-ending, aperistaltic, non-compressible tubular structure (luminal diameter ≈ 10 mm) arising from the caecum at the appendectomy bed. The wall was thickened with surrounding echogenic fat. A minimal amount of free fluid was also observed.
 
 In contrast-enhanced computer tomography (CT), multiplanar reformats demonstrated an enhancing tubular remnant arising from the caecum, capped by two metallic clips at its distal end (Figures 2a, 2b, and 3). The stump showed concentric mural thickening, pericaecal fat stranding, and a trace of free fluid. No evidence of abscess or pneumoperitoneum was identified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 8-year-old male patient presented with chronic left lumbar discomfort, claudication, anaemia (Hb = 8 g/dL), and hyperleukocytosis (12000/mm3). He has been receiving therapy for anaemia for two years.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (US) shows multiple thick-walled collections with echogenic fluid content occupying the left renal fossa and loss of renal parenchyma’s architecture with no corticomedullary differentiation. The thin remaining peripheric parenchymal bundle shows no limits with the thick-walled collections. There is a central pyelic stone measuring 20 mm with a posterior acoustic shadow and a hyperechoic aspect of the perirenal fat.
 
 CT (computed tomography) shows left renal enlargement with destruction of parenchymal architecture and dilated calyces consistent with the “bear paw sign” (Figure 1a), coexisting blurring of perirenal fat, psoas muscle enlargement and abscess formation (Figure 1b), as well as nephroureterolithiasis (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). The left kidney was nonfunctional at the excretory phase scan (Figures 1e and 1f).
 
 On renal scintigraphy (Figure 2), DTPA injection demonstrates reduced relative renal function on the left (17.7% left and 82.3% right), with pyelocaliceal stasis at the excretory phase. Reduced cortical uptake, particularly in the left upper pole, was seen both at DMSA and DTPA scans.

- CT: left renal enlargement with destroyed parenchyma, dilated calyces with "bear paw sign," perirenal fat blurring, psoas muscle involvement with abscesses, nonfunctional left kidney at excretory phase, nephroureterolithiasis.  
- Renal scintigraphy: markedly reduced left renal function (17.7%), pyelocaliceal stasis, reduced cortical uptake, especially upper pole.

- Renal fossa tumour: 
  - Pros: Could explain renal mass and possible pain.
  - Cons: No discrete mass or tumor features such as enhancement, vascularity, or solid tumor characteristics on CT; presence of thick-walled collections and stone point away from tumor; inflammation and abscesses not typical for tumor.  
- Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (diffuse form): 
  - Pros: Classic "bear paw sign," renal enlargement with destruction, multiple thick-walled collections with echogenic fluid (xanthogranulomatous tissue often appears hypoechoic/hyperechoic), large pelvic stone, perirenal and psoas muscle abscesses, nonfunctioning kidney, chronic infection (leukocytosis, anemia), young age not typical but can occur.
  - Cons: Rare in children, but possible; no mention of cholesterol crystals or typical xanthogranulomatous tissue, but imaging findings fit well.  
- Pyonephrosis:

  - Cons: Does not typically cause so extensive renal destruction or the "bear paw sign"; usually limited to infected hydronephrosis; kidney may be less enlarged and less parenchymal loss.  
- Hydronephrosis: 
  - Pros: Dilated calyces, renal enlargement.
  - Cons: No infection or abscess features; collections and abscesses absent; "bear paw sign" not typical; stone alone can cause hydronephrosis but other signs point to infection/inflammation.  
- Urogenital tuberculosis: 
  - Pros: Can cause renal parenchymal destruction and hydronephrosis.
  - Cons: Typically no large stone; abscess and psoas involvement less common; systemic signs more chronic infection without acute abscess; imaging findings not classic for TB.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (diffuse form) – most consistent with "bear paw sign," renal destruction, multiple collections, stone, perirenal and psoas abscesses, and clinical infection signs.  
2) Pyonephrosis – some overlapping signs but less extensive destruction and no "bear paw sign."  
3) Urogenital tuberculosis – less likely given stone and abscesses.  
4) Hydronephrosis – limited to dilated system without infection or abscess features.  
5) Renal fossa tumour – least likely given inflammatory features and abscesses.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (diffuse form)  
</final><|return|>

 A new CTAP revealed that the previous nodule had grown to a size of 6.4 x 6 x 6.7 cm (Figure 2). A chest CT with contrast was unremarkable.
 
 An MRI was ordered for further characterisation of the mass. T1 MRI visualised a heterogeneous enhancing left adrenal mass that was suspicious of malignancy (Figure 3). The size of the lesion was consistent compared to the CTAP.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old healthy male presents with three months of left shoulder pain during activities, followed by a gradual restriction in arm abduction. He denies any trauma, fever, or involvement of other joints. Physical examination reveals swelling in the deltoid region and an inability to raise the left arm to 90°.
 Imaging Findings: Left shoulder x-ray (Figure 1) revealed a mixed sclerotic-lytic lesion involving the proximal diaphysis and metaphysis of the humerus, characterised by poorly defined margins, a wide transition zone, permeative osteolysis, cortical thinning, and a spiculated periosteal reaction with Codman’s triangle, consistent with a Bone-RADS 4 lesion.
 
 MRI demonstrated a large lobulated mass centred in the proximal humerus, hypointense on T1-weighted images (Figure 2a), hyperintense on T2 (Figure 2b) and STIR (Figures 2c and 2d) sequences, with associated extraosseous soft tissue extension and glenohumeral joint effusion. Post-contrast fat-suppressed T1-weighted imaging (Figure 2e) showed heterogeneous enhancement with central non-enhancing areas. Diffusion-weighted imaging (Figure 2f) revealed restricted diffusion with corresponding low signal on the ADC map (Figure 2g) indicating high cellularity.
 
 PET/CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) confirmed an intraosseous mass with osteoid matrix and aggressive periosteal reaction, demonstrating hypermetabolic activity in the proximal humerus, adjacent soft tissues, and secondary foci in the lungs and spinal canal.

- Age: adolescent (peak age for conventional osteosarcoma and chondroblastic subtype).  
- Location: proximal humerus metaphysis/diaphysis, which is common for osteosarcoma but less common for chondroblastoma (typically epiphysis of long bones).

- Pros: Most common subtype of conventional osteosarcoma; occurs in adolescents; aggressive lesion with mixed lytic-sclerotic pattern, permeative destruction, wide transition zone, aggressive periosteal reaction with Codman's triangle; soft tissue mass; high cellularity on diffusion MRI; metastases consistent with osteosarcoma; location in proximal humerus metaphysis/diaphysis matches typical osteosarcoma sites.  
- Cons: None significant; imaging and clinical picture is classic.

- Pros: Aggressive primary bone tumor in adolescents; typical imaging features including mixed lytic-sclerotic lesion, periosteal reaction, soft tissue extension; metastases common.

4) Chondroblastoma (does not match aggressive behavior or imaging features)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 83-year-old gentleman presented to the Urosurgery department with a history of lower urinary tract symptoms in the form of increased frequency and incomplete evacuation of urine. No history of haematuria, burning micturition or fever. On digital rectal examination, the prostate gland was felt to be enlarged with nodularity within felt. On biochemical evaluation, prostate specific antigen (PSA) was marginally raised.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound examination, an enlarged prostate with a volume of 36 cc. There were features of chronic bladder outlet obstruction seen in the form of thickening of the bladder wall (maximum thickness of 8 mm) and a few trabeculations in the bladder wall.
 
 In view of the elevated PSA levels, an MRI prostate was done, which revealed a well-defined, solid, irregularly shaped lesion measuring 3.5 x 5.4 x 5.7 cm (AP x TR x CC) arising from the prostatic urethra and causing its widening and extending into the bladder. This lesion was homogeneously hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted and T2-weighted fat-saturated images (Figures 1 and 2). It was showing homogeneous restriction of diffusion on DWI (Figures 3a and 3b) and was heterogeneously enhancing on post-contrast images. Also, multiple well-defined T2 heterogeneous nodules were seen in the transitional zone (Figure 4). No T2 hypointense or diffusion-restricting lesion was seen in the prostate.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 74-year-old female undergoing immunosuppressive therapy was referred to the orthopaedic department for evaluation of a progressively enlarging mass on the medial side of the left knee, evolving over six months. Initially asymptomatic, the lesion became painful. Notably, she had undergone an articular puncture one year earlier.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with a large mass on the medial aspect of the left knee (Figures 1a and 1b), which was also evident on plain radiography (Figure 2).
 
 Ultrasonography demonstrated a sizable extra-articular heterogeneous lesion with no internal vascularisation, accompanied by a moderate joint effusion and synovial hypertrophy (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed a large extra-articular collection, hyperintense on proton density-weighted fat-suppressed sequences (Figures 4a and 4b) and hypointense on T1-weighted sequences (Figure 4c), with extensive peripheral enhancement following gadolinium administration (Figures 5a and 5b) and no enhancement of solid components. Additionally, MRI revealed extensive synovitis and bone marrow oedema, along with juxta-articular and intra-articular erosions exhibiting peripheral contrast enhancement. No blooming artefacts were identified (Figure 5c).

   Pros: Immunosuppressed host; chronic, slowly progressive mass; extra-articular collection with peripheral enhancement typical for tuberculous-like lesions caused by nontuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) such as M. avium; history of articular puncture (risk factor for infection).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male presented with headache, giddiness, disorientation, and right lower limb weakness. He had a recent history of a fall.
 Imaging Findings: Initial MRI revealed a crescent-shaped extra-axial lesion along the right frontoparietotemporal convexity, hyperintense on T1 and FLAIR-weighted sequences (Figures 1a and 1b), with mild mass effect and midline shift, suggesting a subacute subdural haematoma. A similar extra-axial collection was seen on the left side. No diffusion restriction was observed on DWI (Figure 1c). A well-defined enhancing lesion was also seen in the right lateral ventricle (persistent in serial imaging).
 
 The patient was initially managed conservatively and scheduled for follow-up. Two months later, a non-contrast CT was performed due to recurrence of symptoms, including disorientation and right lower limb weakness. CT revealed a well-defined subdural extraxial crescent-shaped collection along the cerebral convexity in the left frontoparietal region. It was causing a mass effect on the underlying cerebral parenchyma. Midline shift was noted towards the right, with left uncal herniation and dilatation of the contralateral lateral ventricle. A heterogeneous predominantly iso to hypodense lesion was seen along the left frontoparietal convexity, causing mass effect on the underlying brain parenchyma and left lateral ventricle (Figure 2a). Mild periosteal reaction along the inner table of the left parietal bone was observed (Figure 2b).
 
 No update MRI with gadolinium contrast was performed between this CT and surgery. This may have limited diagnostic clarity and contributed to incomplete preoperative planning.
 
 Intraoperatively, no haematoma was identified. Instead, thickened dura, granular periosteum and hyperostosis of underlying bone were noted, raising concern for an underlying neoplastic process (Figure 3).
 
 Postoperative contrast-enhanced MRI revealed a well-defined extra-axial lesion in the left frontoparietotemporal region. It was hyperintense on T2/FLAIR, isointense on T1, with mild diffusion restriction and heterogeneous peripheral enhancement (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c and 4d). There was also a significant mass effect on the adjacent brain parenchyma and ventricles. T1 hyperintense and T2 hypointense areas likely represented postoperative changes. A thin underlying T2 hyperintense, T1 hypointense, and non-enhancing collection were also noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old male presented with worsening lower back pain for 6 months. On physical examination there was no deformity or neurological deficits noted. There was no history of fever, night sweats or weight loss.
 Imaging Findings: An anteroposterior (AP) and lateral radiograph (Figures 1a and 1b) of the lumbar spine was done initially. It showed reduced intervertebral disc space at the L2–L3 level with adjacent end plate erosions and sclerosis, and soft tissue opacity in the paravertebral region at the L2–L3 lumbar vertebral level. Loss of the psoas muscle outline was also noted.
 
 On MRI, partial destruction of the posteroinferior aspect of L1 and posterosuperior aspect of L2 is seen with involvement of the intervening IV disc (Figures 2a and 2b). Altered signal intensity is seen in the vertebral bodies of L4 and L5, which appear hypointense on T1-weighted (T1WI) and hyperintense on T2-weighted (T2WI). There is an associated extensive, multiloculated prevertebral and bilateral paravertebral collection seen (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). The soft tissue components appear hypointense on T1WI and hyperintense on T2W images. These show peripheral restriction of diffusion (Figures 7a and 7b). On post-contrast imaging (Figures 5a, 5b, and 6), there is enhancement of the soft tissue components with central necrotic areas and the involved parts of L1 and L2 vertebral bodies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old female presented to the emergency department with acute lower abdominal pain, accompanied by chronic burning sensations and stabbing pain in the right lower leg. Based on the initial clinical presentation, diverticulitis was suspected, and a routine abdominal CT scan was performed for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: CT imaging demonstrated a lipomatous, highly vascularised mass located posterior to the proximal femoral diaphysis of the right hip, with extension into the ischiofemoral space (Figures 1a and 1b). The lesion was predominantly composed of fatty tissue and exhibited intense arterial enhancement, consistent with a hypervascular process.
 
 Subsequent MRI offered further characterisation, identifying a complex intramuscular mass within the right quadratus femoris muscle, composed of both lipomatous (hyperintense on T1-weighted images) and nodular components (Figure 2). The nodular areas appeared hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2 fat-suppressed sequences (Figure 3). These regions demonstrated moderate, heterogeneous contrast enhancement, indicating variable internal vascularity (Figure 4). There were no significant signal voids on T2* sequences, effectively excluding haemorrhage or calcification. The lesion did not invade surrounding structures. However, the adjacent sciatic nerve exhibited focal thickening, which was suggestive of possible irritation due to mass effect or local inflammation (Figure 5).

   - Cons: Predominantly fatty nature, no invasion of surrounding structures, absence of aggressive features or calcification, intramuscular location less typical for sarcoma, sciatic nerve irritation likely due to mass effect, not infiltration.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 15-year-old female patient with right lower limb shortening since childhood was evaluated in consultation for limb lengthening surgery. Her personal history was negative for trauma. On physical examination, she had a clinically measured leg-length discrepancy of 4 cm.
 Imaging Findings: Full-length standing anteroposterior view radiograph (Figure 1a) demonstrated right lower limb shortening secondary to femoral hypoplasia, with normal ossification and no proximal deformity, consistent with Paley type 1A congenital femoral deficiency (CFD). Mild proximal fibular shortening was also noted, compatible with fibular hemimelia (Achterman–Kalamchi type IA).
 
 On the magnified AP radiograph of the right knee (Figure 1b), the femoral intercondylar notch appeared shallow, and the tibial spines were markedly hypoplastic.
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur (anterior drawer sign) and uncovering of the lateral meniscus posterior horn (Figure 2b), findings suggestive of anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) insufficiency. On mid-sagittal fat-suppressed proton-density (PD) and oblique sagittal PD-weighted images (Figures 2c and 2d), the ACL was not visualised. The posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) was present and vertically oriented (positive PCL line sign). No bone marrow oedema was seen, and the menisci were unremarkable.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 57-year-old diabetic man, with 25 years of bilateral lower limb weakness, presented with a 1.5-month history of a mildly painful, firm, non-tender swelling over the left shoulder. Limited shoulder movement, reduced sensation, and a scar noted (Figures 1a and 1b). No trauma or aspiration records.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the left shoulder (Figure 2) showed joint destruction, including humeral head and glenoid resorption, widened joint space, periarticular calcifications, and soft tissue inflammation.
 
 MRI of the left shoulder (Figures 4, 5, 6, 7a, and 7b) revealed extensive bony damage, complete tears in the rotator cuff tendons and complete destruction of the glenoid labrum, glenohumeral ligaments, and biceps-labral anchor. A large, thick-walled fluid collection with peripheral enhancement extended into the intermuscular and intramuscular planes of the rotator cuff muscles.
 
 MRI of the spine (Figures 3a and 3b) showed a syrinx extending from the cervico-medullary junction to the thoracic vertebra at T11, consistent with syringomyelia, and a wedge collapse of the vertebral body of T12 thoracic vertebra, indicating chronic vertebral involvement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old male presented with severe triventricular ventriculomegaly and cortical thinning at 35 weeks on prenatal ultrasound. At birth, he had an unusual phenotype, adducted thumbs and poor spontaneous mobility. Progressive ventriculomegaly required ventriculoperitoneal derivation (VPD). Currently, the patient has severe neurological involvement, flaccid paresis with paroxysms of rigidity and epilepsy.
 Imaging Findings: Transfontanelar US and postnatal MRI at 1 day and 1 week, respectively, show severe triventricular hydrocephaly and large massa intermedia (Figures 1a and 1b), aqueductal stenosis, enlarged quadrigeminal plate, corpus callosum agenesis, vermian hypoplasia, severe cortical thinning and diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d).
 
 The latest MRI, at the age of 3 years, before VPD treatment and mild improvement of ventriculomegaly, demonstrates better visualisation of the previous findings (Figure 3a), in addition to diffuse hypoplasia of cerebral white matter, polymicrogyria (Figures 3b and 3c), diencephalosynapsis (Figure 3d) with diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia in a so-called “butterfly-like pattern” (Figures 3e and 3f), and hypoplastic and poorly differentiated hippocampi.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male with epigastric pain primarily localised to the right upper quadrant, a positive Murphy’s sign, associated with vomiting and hypotension. The patient presented with a fever spike of 38.1ºC as the only infectious clinical sign observed. Among the analytical parameters, there is a marked elevation of C-reactive protein (CRP; 12.8 mg/dL), procalcitonin (29.8 ng/mL), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH; 2488 U/L), and leukocytosis (24×10^9/L) with neutrophilia (18.9×10^9/L). The family reports diarrhoea in the preceding days.
 Imaging Findings: In a portable chest x-ray, a small left pleural effusion and the presence of gas in the right upper quadrant are seen (Figure 1).
 
 An abdominopelvic CT scan was performed with intravenous contrast in the arterial and portal venous phases. The main findings include numerous subcentimeter gas bubbles arranged in two clusters, primarily affecting the right liver lobe, with maximum axial diameters of 80 mm and 69 mm. Free gas bubbles within the parenchyma and perfusion abnormalities in the affected segments are also observed, along with predominantly central periportal oedema (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 3).

- Portable chest X-ray shows small left pleural effusion and gas in the right upper quadrant.

   - Pros: Presence of intraparenchymal gas in liver, systemic infection signs, rapid deterioration, septic picture.

2) Pyogenic liver abscess:  
   - Pros: RUQ pain, fever, leukocytosis, possible gas-forming organisms.  
   - Cons: Typically presents with a well-defined abscess cavity rather than diffuse, multifocal gas bubbles and necrosis; periportal edema and perfusion defects less common.  

3) Hepatic infarction:  
   - Pros: Perfusion abnormalities can be seen.  
   - Cons: No typical intraparenchymal gas; infarctions rarely produce gas; no primary vascular occlusion or risk factors described; clinical picture is infectious rather than ischemic.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Emphysematous hepatitis (best fits gas bubbles, perfusion defects, periportal edema, and systemic infection).  
2) Pyogenic liver abscess (possible, but imaging less typical due to diffuse gas bubbles and necrosis).  
3) Hepatic infarction (least likely due to gas presence and clinical infection).  
The imaging findings of gas bubbles in clusters in the liver parenchyma with perfusion abnormalities and periportal edema in the clinically septic patient point to emphysematous hepatitis.  
</analysis>

Emphysematous hepatitis  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 22-year-old female presented with hallux valgus deformity of both first toes since birth (Figure 1). At the age of 12, she began experiencing difficulty walking and progressive joint stiffness. On physical examination, there was an inability to fully open the temporomandibular joints bilaterally and restricted flexion and abduction of the extremities.
 Imaging Findings: On plain radiographs of the feet, there was absence of the distal phalanges of the first toes and hallux valgus with metatarsophalangeal angles >15°, supporting early skeletal deformities (Figure 2). CT imaging of the cervicothoracic spine demonstrated heterotopic ossification of the supraspinous and posterior nuchal ligaments (Figure 3). Three-dimensional reconstructions showed multiple bridging ossifications forming tubular bone between the iliac bones and the twelfth ribs, as well as ossified extensions from the right iliac bone to the right humerus (Figures 4a and 4b).
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: On plain radiographs of the feet, there was absence of the distal phalanges of the first toes and hallux valgus with metatarsophalangeal angles >15°, supporting early skeletal deformities. CT imaging of the cervicothoracic spine demonstrated heterotopic ossification of the supraspinous and posterior nuchal ligaments. Three-dimensional reconstructions showed multiple bridging ossifications forming tubular bone between the iliac bones and the twelfth ribs, as well as ossified extensions from the right iliac bone to the right humerus.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Clinical: Congenital absence of distal phalanges of both halluces with hallux valgus, progressive joint stiffness, inability to fully open temporomandibular joints bilaterally, restricted flexion and abduction of extremities.  
- Imaging: Absence of distal phalanges of first toes; hallux valgus with metatarsophalangeal angles >15°; heterotopic ossifications in supraspinous and posterior nuchal ligaments on CT; multiple bridging ossifications forming tubular bone connecting iliac bones to twelfth ribs and from right iliac bone to right humerus.  
These findings indicate widespread heterotopic ossification affecting both axial and appendicular skeleton, congenital absence of bone segment, progressive joint restriction, and ligamentous ossification.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH):  
   - Pros: DISH is characterized by flowing ossifications of the anterior longitudinal ligament and bridging syndesmophytes, predominantly in the thoracic spine. It involves ossification of spinal ligaments.  
   - Cons: DISH does not typically cause congenital absence of distal phalanges or progressive joint stiffness from birth. It mainly involves anterior longitudinal ligament, not supraspinatus or posterior nuchal ligaments. Also, DISH does not cause dramatic heterotopic bone bridging distant sites like iliac to humerus or rib to pelvis. No hallux valgus from congenital absence.  
   Conclusion: Findings do not fit DISH well.

2) Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans (commonly known as Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva, FOP):  
   - Pros: Classic FOP presents with congenital malformation of great toes, often with hallux valgus and partial absence or malformation of distal phalanges. This patient has congenital hallux valgus and phalanx absence. FOP is characterized by progressive heterotopic ossification of soft tissues, including muscles, tendons, ligaments, leading to joint restriction. CT findings of bridging heterotopic bone forming tubular structures between distant bones (pelvis to ribs, iliac to humerus) and ligamentous ossification are classic. Inability to open temporomandibular joints and progressive joint restriction are common in FOP. Age of onset and progression fit.  
   - Cons: None significant.

3) Ankylosing spondylitis (AS):  
   - Pros: Causes new bone formation (syndesmophytes) and ankylosis of spinal joints. May involve ligament ossification.  
   - Cons: AS typically does not involve congenital digit anomalies, particularly hallux valgus or absence of distal phalanges. Ligamentous ossification in AS mainly involves anterior longitudinal ligament. No heterotopic bone bridging muscle planes or ligament across distant bones is typical. Temporomandibular involvement is rare. Clinical presentation and imaging findings don't fit well.

C) Ranking:  
1) Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans (FOP) - best matches congenital hallux abnormalities and widespread heterotopic ossification with joint restriction.  
2) Ankylosing spondylitis - less likely due to lack of congenital anomalies and pattern of ossification.  
3) DISH - least likely given clinical and imaging mismatch.  
Tie-breaks favor FOP for pathognomonic congenital hallux anomaly and heterotopic ossification pattern.

</analysis>  
<final>Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old patient with no notable personal or family medical history. The patient has been complaining for the past 3 years of bilateral gonalgia, right shoulder pain, and rachialgia, with more significant functional discomfort in the lower limbs. Clinical examination reveals tenderness upon palpation of the knees and right shoulder.
 Imaging Findings: A radiological assessment was performed (x-rays and full-body scan) revealing multiple exostotic lesions of the dorsolumbar spine, sternoclavicular joints, costal arches, scapulas, as well as in bones of the upper limbs (bilaterally, more marked at the metaphyseal-epiphyseal region of the right proximal humerus), pelvic girdle, and bones of the lower limbs and feet (bilaterally, more marked at the knees).

   - Pros: Metachondromatosis also presents with multiple exostoses and chondromatous lesions.
   - Cons: Typically, metachondromatosis has a milder exostosis burden and includes enchondroma-like lesions as well as osteochondromas; more common in adolescents but often asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic; may show lesions that regress spontaneously; no mention of cartilage tumors or enchondromas here.
   
2) Solitary osteochondroma
   - Pros: Osteochondroma is the most common benign bone tumor.
   - Cons: By definition, solitary osteochondroma presents as a single exostosis; here there are multiple lesions extensively involving many bones; solitary osteochondroma does not explain multiple lesions.

3) Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva (FOP)
   - Pros: FOP patients have heterotopic ossifications.

   - Pros: Classic presentation of multiple osteochondromas/ exostoses, especially in the metaphyseal and epiphyseal regions of long bones, bilateral and asymmetric; commonly presents before the second decade; typical sites include scapula, pelvic girdle, distal tibia/fibula, proximal humerus/femur, ribs and vertebrae; bilateral knee involvement is common; clinical findings consistent.

   - Pros: Leads to multiple enchondromas.

   - Pros: Maffucci involves multiple enchondromas and soft tissue hemangiomas.

Therefore, the best fit is Multiple hereditary exostoses.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 5-year-old girl presented with a progressively enlarging occipital swelling and mild pain. She had prior right-eye surgeries for eyelid coloboma, with corneal opacification of uncertain cause. She had no light perception in the right eye and reduced vision in the left. No systemic issues. Siblings were healthy.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT revealed a 2.8 cm bony defect at the posterior fontanelle, through which a partially calcified lipomatous lesion was herniating into the subcutaneous scalp (Figure 1a). This lesion extended intracranially along the posterior falx, causing indentation of the superior sagittal sinus (Figure 1b). A second linear lipoma (10 × 2 mm) was seen at the splenium of the corpus callosum (Figure 1c). A 3D volume-rendered reconstruction of the cranial defect shows the 2.8 cm bony defect at the posterior fontanelle (Figure 1d).
 
 MRI confirmed T1-hyperintense lipomas at the posterior fontanelle and splenium, with an extracranial component (Figure 2a). T2-weighted MRI revealed an arachnoid cyst at the left cerebellopontine angle (Figure 2b), and confirmed bilateral colobomas and absence of the right lens (Figure 2c).
PostDescription:

DifferentialDiagnosisList: Oculocerebrocutaneous syndrome (Delleman syndrome), Haberland syndrome (Encephalocraniocutaneous lipomatosis), Linear nevus sebaceous syndrome (Schimmelpenning syndrome), Goldenhar syndrome (Oculoauriculovertebral spectrum)

A) Key findings:  
- 5-year-old girl with posterior fontanelle bony defect (2.8 cm) allowing extracranial extension of a partially calcified lipomatous lesion.  
- Bilateral ocular findings: right-eye corneal opacification, no light perception, left eye reduced vision, bilateral colobomas, absence of right lens.  
- Intracranial lipomas: one extending along the posterior falx causing indentation of the superior sagittal sinus; a second linear lipoma at the splenium of corpus callosum.  
- Presence of a left cerebellopontine angle arachnoid cyst.  
- Extracranial lipomatous lesion herniating through skull defect at posterior fontanelle.  
- No systemic issues or affected siblings.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Oculocerebrocutaneous syndrome (Delleman syndrome):  
   - Pros: Classic triad includes (1) neuroectodermal malformations (e.g., intracranial lipomas, agenesis or hypoplasia of structures), (2) skin anomalies (lipomas), and (3) ocular abnormalities (coloboma, microphthalmia).  
   - Cons: Typically presents with midline scalp defects and facial skin appendages (often absent here). However, posterior fontanelle osteomeningothelial hamartomas are characteristic. The presence of bony skull defect and extracranial lipoma herniating is typical.  
2) Haberland syndrome (Encephalocraniocutaneous lipomatosis):  
   - Pros: Skin and CNS lipomas with unilateral facial and ocular anomalies (e.g., epibulbar dermoids). Often unilateral and hemispheric brain abnormalities.  
   - Cons: Usually involves frontotemporal encephalographic and cutaneous lesions, not classic posterior fontanelle defect or bilateral colobomas. Absence of encephalographic lipomas here. MRI findings less characteristic.  
3) Linear nevus sebaceous syndrome (Schimmelpenning syndrome):  
   - Pros: Sebaceous nevi of scalp, CNS anomalies, seizures.  
   - Cons: Does not involve skull defects or intracranial lipomas; ocular colobomas and lens absence rare.  
4) Goldenhar syndrome (Oculoauriculovertebral spectrum):  
   - Pros: Ocular coloboma and facial anomalies common.  
   - Cons: No intracranial lipoma or skull defects typical; usually facial asymmetry and ear anomalies, not skull defects or lipomas.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Oculocerebrocutaneous syndrome (Delleman) – fits best with posterior fontanelle skull defect, extracranial lipomatous herniation, intracranial lipomas, bilateral ocular colobomas, and no systemic/genetic siblings.  
2) Haberland syndrome – less typical due to bilateral colobomas and posterior fontanelle defect not characteristic.  
3) Goldenhar syndrome – ocular coloboma fits but lacks CNS lipomas or skull defect.  
4) Linear nevus sebaceous syndrome – least fitting; no sebaceous nevi or typical CNS lipomas.  

Thus, the findings are most classic for Oculocerebrocutaneous syndrome (Delleman syndrome).  
</analysis>  
<final>Oculocerebrocutaneous syndrome (Delleman syndrome)</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old male presented to the emergency department at our institution with the chief complaints of insidious, throbbing frontal headache, dizziness, photo-phonophobia and emesis for one week. Neurological examination revealed a positive Kernig sign, ataxia and impaired finger-to-nose testing.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head demonstrated a diffuse, heterogeneous solid-cystic lesion in the right inferomedial cerebellar hemisphere involving the adjacent vermis. The solid component was fairly defined with irregular margins, manifesting as mild expansion of the cerebellar folia with abnormal low T1 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) and high T2 FLAIR signal interspersed with thick T2 dark striations (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). The lobulated cystic component showed high signal on T2WI, intermediate on T1WI and incomplete FLAIR suppression without diffusion restriction.
 
 Post-contrast images demonstrated nodular and linear enhancement within the folia and peripheral enhancement of the cyst (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). Mass effect in the posterior fossa included mild perilesional oedema and partial effacement of the fourth ventricle. There was no evident enlargement of supratentorial ventricles or periventricular oedema. We observed an inferior cerebellar tonsillar descent of 7 mm.
 
 CT showed thick, linear striated calcifications mimicking a “tiger-striped” appearance. Bony remodelling with scalloping of the overlying occipital bone suggested an indolent long-standing lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old male presented to the emergency department at our institution with the chief complaints of insidious, throbbing frontal headache, dizziness, photo-phonophobia and emesis for one week. Neurological examination revealed a positive Kernig sign, ataxia and impaired finger-to-nose testing.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head demonstrated a diffuse, heterogeneous solid-cystic lesion in the right inferomedial cerebellar hemisphere involving the adjacent vermis. The solid component was fairly defined with irregular margins, manifesting as mild expansion of the cerebellar folia with abnormal low T1 and high T2 FLAIR signal interspersed with thick T2 dark striations. The lobulated cystic component showed high signal on T2WI, intermediate on T1WI and incomplete FLAIR suppression without diffusion restriction. Post-contrast images demonstrated nodular and linear enhancement within the folia and peripheral enhancement of the cyst. Mass effect in the posterior fossa included mild perilesional oedema and partial effacement of the fourth ventricle. There was no evident enlargement of supratentorial ventricles or periventricular oedema. We observed an inferior cerebellar tonsillar descent of 7 mm. CT showed thick, linear striated calcifications mimicking a “tiger-striped” appearance. Bony remodelling with scalloping of the overlying occipital bone suggested an indolent long-standing lesion.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma, Pilocytic astrocytoma, Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma, MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion), Oligodendroglioma-like tumour
FinalDiagnosis: Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma, MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion)

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 11-year-old male with subacute neurological symptoms suggesting increased intracranial pressure and cerebellar involvement (ataxia, Kernig sign indicating possible meningitis or irritation of meninges).  
- MRI: Diffuse heterogeneous solid-cystic lesion in the right inferomedial cerebellar hemisphere extending to adjacent vermis.  
- Solid component: mildly expanding cerebellar folia, low T1, high T2/FLAIR signal with thick T2 dark striations.  
- Cystic component: high T2, intermediate T1, incomplete FLAIR suppression, no diffusion restriction.  
- Post-contrast: nodular and linear enhancement within folia, peripheral cyst enhancement only.  
- Mass effect: mild perilesional edema, partial fourth ventricle effacement, no supratentorial hydrocephalus.  
- CT: thick linear striated calcifications with a "tiger-striped" appearance; bony remodeling and occipital scalloping indicating slow-growing lesion.  
- Clinical context: young patient, posterior fossa lesion, slow growth with calcifications and bone remodeling.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma (Lhermitte-Duclos disease)  
- Pros: Age fits (children/adolescents), classic tiger-striped pattern on MRI due to enlarged dysplastic cerebellar folia, thick linear T2 hypointense striations, expansion of folia with preserved architecture, usually solid cerebellar lesion. Calcifications possible but less common. Often no solid-cystic components. No enhancement typical.  
- Cons: Presence of solid-cystic components with peripheral enhancement and nodular enhancement not typical. CT calcifications not classic. Tonsillar herniation mild but possible. Bony remodeling uncommon.  

2) Pilocytic astrocytoma  
- Pros: Most common posterior fossa tumor in children, often cystic with solid mural nodule, peripheral cyst enhancement, nodular solid enhancement, usually no significant calcifications but can occur. Can cause bone remodeling. Slow-growing.  
- Cons: CALCIIFICATION is unusual (but possible), thick linear calcifications and tiger-striped appearance is not typical. Dark T2 striations not common. Typically well-defined solid nodule, not diffuse solid component.  

3) Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma  
- Pros: WHO entity defined by diffuse growth pattern, often lacks well-defined margin, can involve cerebellum, shows T2 hyperintense lesion, can be solid or cystic, may cause mild edema, often shows minimal or no enhancement. Usually no calcifications.  
- Cons: Presence of thick calcifications and tiger-striped appearance is unusual. Enhancement pattern with nodular and linear enhancement within folia is not classic. Bone remodeling is uncommon.  

4) MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion)  
- Pros: Molecular phenotype more than radiologic entity, could correlate with some pediatric cerebellar tumors, e.g. pilocytic astrocytoma-like or low-grade gliomas. However, the description seems to refer to a molecular subgroup rather than a morphologic diagnosis.  
- Cons: No specific imaging hallmark; more of a genetic classification. Not likely to be selected purely on imaging.  

5) Oligodendroglioma-like tumour  
- Pros: Rare in posterior fossa, pediatric variants exist; may show calcifications; T2 bright and variable.  
- Cons: Oligodendrogliomas are rare in children and posterior fossa; typical supratentorial location; calcifications are common but "tiger-striped" pattern and cerebellar folial expansion not typical. Does not fit the age or site well.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma (Lhermitte-Duclos disease) fits best with tiger-striped T2 dark striations, cerebellar folial expansion, solid lesion. However, the presence of cystic component with peripheral cyst enhancement and nodular solid enhancement, calcifications, and bone remodeling suggest a more aggressive lesion.  
2) Pilocytic astrocytoma: cystic lesion with solid mural nodule and peripheral cyst enhancement fits, but calcifications are uncommon, and bone remodeling is rare though possible. T2 dark striations are not typical, but overall morphology and location favor it.  
3) Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma: consistent with diffuse growth and non-enhancing lesion but calcifications and bone erosion push it down.  
4) Oligodendroglioma-like tumour: poor fit due to age and location, calcifications possible but other features not typical.  
5) MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion): too vague and molecular; no imaging specifics.  

Considering all features, especially the tiger-striped calcifications, solid-cystic nature with peripheral cyst enhancement, and bony remodeling, the best fit is a pilocytic astrocytoma with unusual calcifications. However, the striking T2 dark striations and folial expansion are classic for Lhermitte-Duclos disease. But the cystic component with peripheral enhancement and nodular solid enhancement is more typical of pilocytic astrocytoma. The "tiger-striped" calcifications can be seen in pilocytic astrocytomas rarely. The slow growth and bone remodeling also favor pilocytic astrocytoma. Therefore, the diagnosis of Pilocytic astrocytoma is preferred.  

<final>Pilocytic astrocytoma</final><|return|>
options: Dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma | Pilocytic astrocytoma | Paediatric-type diffuse low-grade glioma | MAP kinase pathway altered (FGFR1::TACC1 fusion) | Oligodendroglioma-like tumour

Bilateral symmetric high signal in DWI and T2/FLAIR with corresponding low signal in ADC are seen in the bilateral caudate nucleus, globus pallidus and bilateral posteromedial thalamus (Figures 3a, 3b, and 4).

   Pros: Can present with neurocognitive decline and behavior changes; sometimes shows cortical and basal ganglia involvement.  
   Cons: Usually less commonly associated with such pronounced cortical ribboning on DWI with low ADC; often involves limbic system; myoclonus and basal ganglia involvement less typical; lack of typical imaging features such as limbic hyperintensity or gyriform enhancement.

   Pros: Basal ganglia and diffuse cortical involvement is possible in metabolic derangements; typically symmetric basal ganglia changes.  
   Cons: Usually affects bilateral symmetric regions diffusely and not focal asymmetrical cortical ribboning; clinical presentation with myoclonus and progressive cognitive decline is less typical; no mention of systemic metabolic abnormalities.  

3) Hypoxic/anoxic brain injury  
   Pros: Can cause cortical ribboning with restricted diffusion and basal ganglia involvement due to hypoxic injury.  
   Cons: Usually seen after a clinical event of hypoxia; no history of hypoxic event; basal ganglia involvement often bilateral symmetric but cortical ribboning in this pattern and subacute timeline less typical.  

4) Osmotic demyelination  
   Pros: Affects basal ganglia and thalami.  
   Cons: Typically involves pontine and extrapontine areas with T2 hyperintensity but no cortical ribboning; DWI changes less typical; clinical presentation does not fit.  

5) Mitochondrial disease  
   Pros: Can involve basal ganglia and cortex with DWI changes; sometimes shows restricted diffusion.  
   Cons: Usually younger patients or chronic progressive disease; imaging findings less characteristic; no mention of systemic features or genetic history.

   Pros: Classic DWI cortical ribboning with low ADC in cortical areas and basal ganglia (caudate, putamen, thalami); subacute rapidly progressive dementia with myoclonus; typical involvement of right frontoparieto-temporal cortices and medial thalami; basal ganglia involvement is symmetric; clinical picture fits well.  
   Cons: None significant; imaging and clinical picture is classic.

5) Mitochondrial disease (less typical age and presentation).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old woman taking etanercept for psoriatic arthritis presented to the emergency department with pain in her right wrist for three weeks, predominantly in the fourth and fifth fingers. She reported no history of trauma or prior procedures. Clinical examination revealed swelling on the palmar surface and pain with movement. An ultrasound was performed, which showed synovial fluid (images not available).
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the right hand showed fluid in the synovial sheath of the flexor tendons (Figure 1a), extending from the distal radioulnar joint to the metacarpals, with multiple small nodules within the tendon sheath, ranging from hypointense to isointense, consistent with “rice bodies” (Figures 1b, 1c, and 1d). Soft tissue enlargement and thickening of the flexor sheath tendons were also observed (Figures 1e and 1f).
 
 Two months after starting treatment, a follow-up MRI was performed, which showed the disappearance of the “rice bodies” with a slight persistence of synovial fluid in the flexor tendon sheath (Figures 2a and 2b). A decrease in soft tissue enlargement and flexor sheath thickening was also observed (Figure 2c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 35-year-old gentleman presented with a gradually progressive headache since 6 months, which was eventually accompanied by visual disturbance, giddiness and difficulty in walking. There was no history of trauma or any other systemic complaints. Fundoscopic examination was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced MRI brain revealed a 3.1 x 3.4 x 3.5 cm-sized T2 hypointense (Figure 1), and thick-walled, ring-enhancing (Figure 2) lesion in the left hemicerebellum. The lesion showed T1 isointense signal (Figure 3) without any diffusion restriction (Figures 4a and 4b). Associated, there was significant perilesional oedema and mass effect causing compression of the fourth ventricle (Figure 1) with resultant moderate upstream hydrocephalus and periventricular cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) seepage. On MR spectroscopy, the lesion showed a prominent lipid lactate peak with reduced choline and N-acetylaspartate levels (Figures 5a and 5b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 An 8-year-old boy presented with recurrent focal seizures since infancy and progressive developmental delay, along with a port-wine stain on the right side of his face, present since birth.
 
 Case 2
 
 A 54-year-old male with a right hemifacial port-wine stain presented with long-standing epilepsy, progressive left-hemiplegia, cognitive decline and migraine-like headaches. Ophthalmologic evaluation revealed visual field defects and glaucoma.
 Imaging Findings: Case 1
 
 Non-contrast CT images of the brain revealed gyriform, curvilinear cortical and subcortical calcifications along the right parieto-occipital region, with a “tram-track” appearance and associated mild cortical atrophy (Figure 1a). Post-contrast CT images demonstrated pial enhancement over the right parieto-occipital cortex, consistent with pial angiomas. There was also enlargement and enhancement of the ipsilateral choroid plexus (Figure 1b). Additionally, homogeneous crescentic enhancement of the choroid was found along the posterior segment of the right globe, consistent with a choroidal angioma (Figure 1c).
 
 Case 2
 
 T2-weighted and FLAIR axial MR images show right cerebral volume loss with diffusely thinned cortex, ex vacuo dilatation of ipsilateral lateral ventricle, and extensive curvilinear cortical hypointensity (Figures 2a and 2b). Axial susceptibility-weighted image (SWI) shows “blooming” of the extensive cortical/subcortical calcifications on the right cerebral hemisphere (Figure 2c). Post-contrast T1-weighted image shows serpentine gyriform enhancement, and enlargement and enhancement of choroid plexus (Figure 2d).

- Pial enhancement and serpentine gyriform enhancement post-contrast.  
- Enlarged and enhancing ipsilateral choroid plexus.  
- Both cases have ipsilateral ocular and cerebral vascular malformations/angiomas.  
  
B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Meningioangiomatosis  
   - Pros: This rare entity often presents with cortical and subcortical calcifications and leptomeningeal enhancement.  
   - Cons: Usually not associated with facial port-wine stains or ocular angiomas; no typical choroid plexus involvement; typically sporadic or associated with neurofibromatosis type 2, not presenting with facial vascular malformations.  
2) Blue rubber bleb nevus syndrome  
   - Pros: Involves venous malformations and can have seizures.  
   - Cons: Typically presents with multiple venous malformations of skin and GI tract; not characterized by facial port-wine stains, leptomeningeal angiomas, or calcifications; no choroidal or choroid plexus lesions described.  
3) Wyburn-Mason syndrome  
   - Pros: Associated with retinal and cerebral arteriovenous malformations; sometimes seizures.  
   - Cons: No port-wine stain; typically has high-flow AVMs rather than leptomeningeal or choroidal angiomas; imaging findings differ; no cortical calcifications or choroid plexus involvement.  
4) Sturge-Weber syndrome, type 1  
   - Pros: Classic facial port-wine stain (unilateral), leptomeningeal angiomas with gadolinium enhancement, choroidal angioma, ipsilateral choroid plexus enlargement and enhancement, cortical calcifications with tram-track appearance, seizures, and progressive neurologic deficits; ocular involvement with glaucoma and visual field defects is common.  
   - Cons: None contradictory.  
5) PHACE syndrome  
   - Pros: Related to large facial hemangiomas, arterial cerebrovascular anomalies.  
   - Cons: Usually involves posterior fossa malformations and large segmental hemangiomas, not leptomeningeal angiomas or choroidal angiomas; no typical cortical calcifications or choroid plexus enhancement; also, usually a female predilection and often diagnosed in infants/young children.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Sturge-Weber syndrome, type 1 â pathognomonic combination of facial port-wine stain, leptomeningeal angioma, choroidal angioma, cortical calcifications, and choroid plexus enhancement.  
2) Wyburn-Mason syndrome â some overlap with cerebral vascular malformations but lacks port-wine stain and leptomeningeal/choroidal angiomas.  
3) PHACE syndrome â less typical given lack of posterior fossa involvement and no leptomeningeal angioma.  
4) Meningioangiomatosis â possible but no systemic angiomas or port-wine stain.  
5) Blue rubber bleb nevus syndrome â not consistent with neuroimaging findings here.  
Thus, the diagnosis is unequivocally Sturge-Weber syndrome, type 1.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 : A 9-year-old girl presented to the otorhinolaryngology (ENT) department with profound hearing loss. On evaluation, she was detected to have right-sided sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL).
 
 Case 2
 
 : A 6-year-old boy was referred from the ENT department with a clinical diagnosis of severe right-sided SNHL.
 
 Case 3
 
 : A 2-year-old child was referred with a clinical diagnosis of right-sided SNHL for MRI.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain were performed, and the most relevant findings for each case are noted below:
 
 Case 1
 
 : Thinned-out cochlear division of the right vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII) (Figure 1b). Stenosed right internal auditory meatus (IAM) (Figure 1a). The facial nerve CN VII appeared normal.
 
 Case 2
 
 : Non-visualised cochlear division of the right CN VIII (Figure 2a). CN VII appeared normal.
 
 Case 3
 
 : The cochlear nerve is not visualised in the anteroinferior aspect of the right IAM (Figure 3a).
